I use a wrapper around gabbi that, amongst other things, helps to handle request authentication throughout the suite of e2e tests. Specifically, this wrapper sets variables in `$ENVIRON` which are read from a config file. It's then possible to dynamically provide the required authentication token in a request header.

Up until recently, the header key hasn't changed, but now I am needing to extend the wrapper to read the header key from the config as well so it can by dynamically changed in the tests. 

Overall it provides a bit more flexibility with the test suite in being able to run it with different authentication methods and tokens.

代码里面有如下的正则表达式，用来提取中文
> re_han_default = re.compile("([\u4E00-\u9FD5a-zA-Z0-9+#&\._%]+)", re.U)
也就是说对于”弗里德里克·泰勒”中的“·”不认为是中文，而是作为类似逗号和句号来处理。

一个不太好但是可以用的办法就是，修改上面的正则表达式，将“·”加入。其中\xb7就是“·”。

> re_han_default = re.compile("([\u4E00-\u9FD5a-zA-Z0-9+#&\.\xb7%]+)", re.U)

测试结果：
》 这个 | 外国人 | 叫 | 弗里德里克·泰勒 | ， | 是 | 美国 | 前 | 国防部 | 部长 | 。 | 冯·诺伊曼 | 是 | 公认 | 的 | 计算机 | 发明人

So basically you have a call stack problem. Because you call the next() transition in the one state handler, the fsm is performing a transition "while performing a transition", and the "second", deeper transition completes before the initial transition (because the onone transition blocks until it's done transitioning with next()). Does that make sense?

In other words: onone is actually onenterone. But onenterone is called before onchangestate (you're not really in the new state unless onenter has completed, since you could cancel the transition or do it async).

I think what you want to do here is perform the next() transition in the onafter_initialize. This way the state is changed and onchangestate has been called, which should have the correct order.

Does that help? I guess we should have a section in the callbacks documentation that explains in which callbacks it's safe to call a transition.

~/.config/thefuck/rules/ssh_known_hosts.py
```
import re
from thefuck.utils import for_app

commands = ('ssh', 'scp', 'root')

@for_app(*commands)
def match(command):
    if not command.script:
        return False
    if not command.script.startswith(commands):
        return False

    patterns = (
        r'WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!',
        r'WARNING: POSSIBLE DNS SPOOFING DETECTED!',
        r"Warning: the \S+ host key for '([^']+)' differs from the key for the IP address '([^']+)'",
    )

    return any(re.findall(pattern, command.stderr) for pattern in patterns)

def get_new_command(command):
    return command.script


def side_effect(old_cmd, command):
    offending_pattern = re.compile(
        r'(?:Offending (?:key for IP|\S+ key)|Matching host key) in ([^:]+):(\d+)',
        re.MULTILINE)
    offending = offending_pattern.findall(old_cmd.stderr)
    for filepath, lineno in offending:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as fh:
            lines = fh.readlines()
            del lines[int(lineno) - 1]
        with open(filepath, 'w') as fh:
            fh.writelines(lines)

# Optional:
priority = 5
enabled_by_default = True
```

I’ve decided that this task is simple enough to just use shell scripting to accomplish. It just involves surrounding procedure names in double square brackets.  `*subroutine* **My_sub** …` becomes `*subroutine* **[[My_sub]]** …` which will cause FORD to create a link to it. If no such procedure exists (say because it is an internal procedure) then FORD does not create a link, and the double brackets just get printed in bold with the procedure name. Here is the sed script I use:

```bash
sed -i"bak" -E 's; \*\*([a-zA-Z]+[a-zA-Z0-9_]*)\*\*; \*\*[[\1]]\*\*;' json_module.F90.gcov.md
```

You might be using python3? In this case. here are the instructions for building this project from source:

1. Setup and activate your python3 virtualenv

    virtualenv --no-site-packages -p python3 venv_py3
    source venv_py3/bin/activate

2. Install future.

    pip3 install future

3. Clone the repo and build the source.

    git clone git@github.com:xmunoz/sodapy.git
    cd sodapy/
    git checkout python3
    python3 setup.py install
    python3 setup.py install

That last line is not a typo. For some reason you need to `install` twice, it dies the first time on `requests`, and succeeds the second time. I don't know why...

4. Once it's finished, the binaries should be moved into the correct location (in your virtualenv), so you can clean up the source.
    
    cd ..
    rm -rf sodapy/

I might have gotten too excited to have found a fix. But in retrospect no additions are needed, the additional "mac" item can be left out. This can still be made to work with just the 3 parameters in the string serialize.

Some findings i have made from testing on my TV are listed below.
 Based on your existing payload string:
`payload = b"\x64\x00" \
                  + self._serialize_string(config["description"]) \
                  + self._serialize_string(config["id"]) \
                  + self._serialize_string(config["name"])`

1. "description" - should ideally be the IP address of the device (can be any thing, no enforcement here). This is not affected by change across sessions.
2. "id" - should ideally be the MAC. Again no enforcement. This value can be anything, **BUT should remain constant across sessions**. If it is changed TV will ask for re-auth.
3. "name" - Is the name of the remote. This remains as is and is also not affected by change across sessions.

So i guess the short is - a little clean up of naming of the payload components and the example should clear this up for all the folks having re-auth issues.

Hi @koalamon! By default `zap-cli` will only output "High" alerts that are found (i.e. XSS, CSRF, SQLi, etc.). If you also want to output lower level alerts like the X-Frame-Options header being missing, you can use the `--alert-level` or `-l` parameter with the `quick-scan` command (which can be either High, Medium, Low, Informational).

So, if you want to output all alerts, you could use "Informational" with your command:
```
docker run -i owasp/zap2docker-stable zap-cli quick-scan -l Informational -s all --self-contained --start-options '-config api.disablekey=true' https://www.leankoala.com
```

You can also change that to Low or Medium to output fewer alerts by only including those of a higher severity.

I hope that helps!

Threading doesn't occur on cfitsio level; but cfitsio is compatible with threads (see https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/docs/software/fitsio/c/c_user/node15.html )

Suppose files reside on a 'potentially slow` file-system (eg lustre or gpfs), using multiple threads can allows overlapping computation with IO. 
For example, if I have a list of fits file names, after the patch one can use

```
filename = ['a.fits', 'b.fits', 'c.fits']
import multiprocessing.pool
pool = multiprocessing.pool.ThreadPool()
def process(filename)
    f = fitsio.FITS(filename)
    # process file f
pool.map(filenames)
```

Python cannot take advantage of this unless the GIL is released. This is a well known issue. As an example, most numpy functions releases GIL in the computation core as well. See http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/release.html (More GIL releases) 

Of course multiprocessing can work around the GIL issue too, but it doesn't hurt to release the GIL as much as possible (plus numpy is also doing so as well).

Hi,

It would not shock me to have support for SNMPv1. But implementation may need some refactoring of existing code. I don't like what I did with the external API. I am talking about the fact that you simply do:

    from puresnmp import get
    get(...)

The idea behind this was making it as simple as possible to use. However, this not causes problems with the other protocol versions (v1 and v3). Especially v3 takes a complete different set of arguments aside from the OIDs. So the signature or `puresnmp.get` would become quite messy.

I was thinking about having some kind of factory returning a "client" instance with the `.get`, `.walk`, ... methods. Something along the lines of this:

    from puresnmp import Client
    client = Client.create(Client.V2C, 'community')
    result = client.get(ip, oid)

There are some open questions in my mind though about this:

* Which arguments do you pass to `Client.create` (ip, community, timeout, ...)?
* Is the factory method a good idea? Why not have separate classes? Or move the existing functions to separate modules and completely forego classes?
* ...?

I have not really put much thought into it yet as SNMPv3 is planned, yes, but it is currently low-priority. All devices I am querying are in a secure and isolated network and would only suffer from the SNMPv3 overhead. So migration to v3 is not yet really planned, and as long as that's not the case I am leaving this on the backlog.

If you would like I would more than welcome a proposal for a SNMPv1 implementation!

My primary concerns about the end-result are:

* Is the external API simple?
* Does the external API not force any arguments on the end-user which are unnecessary?
* Is the external API not overly repetitive. The choice of classes, factories and which arguments will be specified where would have a big impact on this point.
* Are the in/out arguments "pythonic"?

Network engineers frequently use IP address (probably majority of the time). 

A lot of the time (most of the time) is there no corresponding DNS name nor DNS alias. You could make a strong argument it would be better to use names, but we usually don't do this.

So how do we differentiate between these two entries?

host 10.10.10.27:9822           # Juniper SRX
  user pyclass
  hostname 10.10.10.27
  Port 9822
  ProxyCommand ssh jumphost nc %h %p

host 10.10.10.27:8022           # Cisco rtr2
  user pyclass
  hostname 10.10.10.27
  Port 8022
  ProxyCommand ssh jumphost nc %h %p

Also Netmiko would then have to know the "name" of the entry in the SSH config (when there is a very good chance it doesn't have this information i.e. only has an ip address and port).

I think this is probably a case where common system practices conflict with common network engineering practices.

Hello Lee,

Thank you for your feedback.  You are using the library correctly - the data just doesn't match up. In this case, there is no experimental data or data fits to provide accurate data for the viscosity of glycerol. There is also no experimental data included for the liquid density of glycerol. This library has attempted to incorporate as many predictive methods as possible for those cases, but liquid viscosity is easily the hardest to predict. The two methods implemented, 
[Letsou_Stiel](https://thermo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/thermo.viscosity.html#thermo.viscosity.Letsou_Stiel)
and [Przedziecki_Sridhar](https://thermo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/thermo.viscosity.html#thermo.viscosity.Przedziecki_Sridhar) provide obscenely low estimates. They are not designed for polar compounds.

Estimation methods will probably never work for chemicals like Glycerol. I would definitely like another source of data for viscosity. Do you know of any good reference works including data for many chemicals? Because I am attempting to have data for such a large number of chemicals, it would be unsustainable for me to add a fit for the single chemical Glycerol, although it would be easy to do. 

In the mean time, you may wish to implement the method described here: [http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.613.9220&rep=rep1&type=pdf
](http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.613.9220&rep=rep1&type=pdf
)
Although I do not have a fix for this issue for you right now, please continue to file any bugs you find. This will help me prioritize what to work on. I will leave the bug open until I have better data for this mixture.

Sincerely,
Caleb

@mwaterfall - I'm having a hard time parsing your problem.  It sounds like you are able to use `parse` to create Delorean objects with a fixed offset (ex. '-0700'). Once Delorean parses your input it converts the time to UTC, so when you want to return a datetime object from the Delorean object you are unable to convert the datetime back to the original fixed offset.

If you knew the name of the timezone that for the datetime, you could convert it using shift, but shift does not understand fixed offset.

In other words, what you would like to do is something like the following:

```python
>>> d = parse('2015-01-01 00:00:00 -0800')
>>> d.shift('-0800')
>>> d.datetime
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=pytz.FixedOffset(-480))
```

The other option I can imagine is a new class property that returns a localized datetime object, like this:

```python
>>> d = parse('2015-01-01 00:00:00 -0800')
>>> d.local_datetime
datetime.datetime(2015, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=pytz.FixedOffset(-480))
```

The first change seems pretty simple, but the second will require a bit more work because Delorean objects will have to remember their local timezone after they've been parsed. I have a feeling you're looking for the functionality in the later.  Can you confirm?

Thanks for the reply! I don't have much familiarity with `sklearn` so it's cool to see someone using `deco` with it. I tried just briefly to get the decorators to work with class methods, but ran into some complications. I unfortunately don't have enough free time at the moment to work through this, but will definitely consider it at a later date. If you're interested here are the main complications:

**Descriptor API and implementing __get__**
Both concurrent and synchronized decorators need to implement `__get__(self, obj, type = None)` methods in order to be used as class methods. That problem lies in being able to return basically a copy of the wrapper object that has a modified `__call__` attribute which will contain the appropriate `self` in it's `*args`.

**More complicated AST parsing**
This is really the hardest problem to solve. The AST parsing has remained really simple so far because deco can expect concurrent functions to be in the global namespace. Once that assumption is gone, detecting which function calls are actually concurrent functions becomes more complicated.

**TL;DR:**
Working with class methods is maybe possible, but definitely hard. I hope to revisit this at a later date, but hopefully you'll still find `deco` useful in the mean time.

Here's the script i am using, it's got other stuff in it (like mailto) because I use it as a handler for other stuff too. works nicely set as $BROWSER with rtv.

    #!/bin/bash
    
    ARGS=$(getopt -o n -- "$@")
    PRE=""
    q=""
    url=""
    eval set -- "$ARGS"
    
    while true; do
        case $1 in
            -n)
                # print command that would be run
                PRE="echo "
                # keep this behaviour if we call ourselves recursively (print the final command that will be run)
                q=" -n"
                shift
            ;;
            --)
                shift
                break
            ;;
        esac
    done
    # let xdg do url scheme handling where appropriate, this script handles special cases (youtube, imgur, etc.)
    
    DEFAULT="${PRE}xdg-open"
    VIDEO="mpv"
    IMAGE="feh -."
    GIF="mpv --loop=inf"
    
    url=$1
    case $url in
        http*://*youtube.com/watch?*|http*://youtu.be/watch?*)
            ${PRE}${VIDEO} "$url"
            ;;
        *.jpg*|*.png*)
            ${PRE}${IMAGE} "$url"
            ;;
        *.gif*)
            ${PRE}${GIF} "${url/.gifv/.webm}"
            ;;
        *imgur.com/a/*|*imgur.com/gallery/*)
            ${DEFAULT} "$url"
            ;;
        *imgur.com/*)
            $0$q $(curl -s "$url" | sed -n 's/^.*<link rel="image_src"\s\+href="\([^"]\+\)".*$/\1/p')
            ;;
        mailto:*)
            ${PRE}urxvt -e mutt -- "${url}"
            ;;
        *)
            ${DEFAULT} "$url"
            ;;
    esac

I think I see why you may have changed it.  With a parser like:
```
parser.add('--word_list', nargs='+', type=str) 
parser.parse_args('--word_list word1 word2 word3 -w config_out_file.txt')
```
The contents of config_out_file.txt using the .join() method are:

> word_list = [word1, word2, word3]

versus without the special handling of lists:
> word_list = ['word1', 'word2', 'word3']

Note the single quotes.  Seems innocuous until you try to load the args back in using config_out_file.txt.
```
args = parser.parse_args('-c config_out_file.txt')
args.word_list  # returns ["'word1'", "'word2 '", "'word3'"]
```
My current work-around is:
```
    def serialize(self, items):
        """Does the inverse of config parsing by taking parsed values and
        converting them back to a string representing config file contents.
        """
        r = StringIO()
        for key, value in items.items():
            if type(value) is list:
                value = [str(item) for item in value]
                value = "["+", ".join(value)+"]"
            r.write("%s = %s\n" % (key, value))
        return r.getvalue()
```
However, this is only relevant after tackling https://github.com/bw2/ConfigArgParse/issues/42
which prevents reading in config settings from file with params that use nargs = '+', '*', or nargs>1.

In the XML, orientation and group are not directly represented.  Instead of orientation there is position (top, bottom, right, left; presumably others for 3d charts), although we could make a lookup to do something like `'v' if position in ['l', 'r'] else 'h'`.  To determine the group, we'll need to look at which axis Ids are associated with the plots in a given chart.  I think the first occurance of an orientation in the chart becomes the primary axis.  If that's true, we would need to iterate over the plots and the first vertical axis referenced in the `c:axId` tags becomes the primary vertical axis.  The next step is to test changing primary and secondary axes by reordering them in plots.  If it works, `.group` should be relatively simple.

So we need to make things a bit more robust and check we always access `self._player` in a way that is safe for dbus throwing an error.

Here's a cumbersomely slow version. Note that unlike your example, I interpret piano rolls as Frame by Notes matrix, not Notes by Frames.

```
def pianoroll_to_midi(pianoroll, fs, program=1, filepath='midifile.mid'):
    # create PrettyMIDI object
    midifile = pm.PrettyMIDI()
    # create Instrument instance
    if type(program) is not int:
        program = pm.instrument_name_to_program(program)
    instrument = pm.Instrument(program=program)

    # cumbersomely slow
    for note in range(pianoroll.shape[1]):
        start = 0
        end = 0
        while end < pianoroll.shape[0]:
            # find where note starts
            while start < pianoroll.shape[0] and pianoroll[start, note] == 0:
                start += 1
            end = start + 1

            # nothing left
            if start == pianoroll.shape[0]:
                break

            # find where note ends
            while end < pianoroll.shape[0] and pianoroll[end, note] > 0:
                end += 1

            # add note to instrument
            instrument.notes.append(pm.Note(
                velocity=int(pianoroll[start, note]),
                pitch=note,
                start=start * 1.0/fs,
                end=end * 1.0/fs))
            start = end

    midifile.instruments.append(instrument)
    midifile.write(filepath)
```

Hey David,

Sorry I missed this. `_changed` essentially was designed to be able to track what leaves would need to be serialised, and similar to the NETCONF `trim` behaviour (see RFC6243) then it doesn't serialise defaults. The idea was to ensure that the minimum output possible is produced when the user does a `.get()` such that elements that are specified in the schema with the same value are specified.

At the moment, it isn't possible to know that something has changed when it is set to the default. A fix for this would be for us to change the `_changed` behaviour to set to true if the value was changed at all, and then have the `.get()` implementation filter this out. We'd probably want to have some new argument that would allow the different modes that we might want. I'd propose to leave `filter=True` doing the same thing as it does today, but then have a `any_change=True` for the mode you suggest. We could also have a `with_defaults` flag which allows any leaf with a default to be serialised.

Any thoughts?

I really like the direction of this PR as it focus on fixing #9, however I'm worried about the implications of starting a `Thread` for each resolved promise.
In a "synchronous" python environment starting a thread might be the path to go, however in environments where `gevent` or `asyncio` (event loops) are used, starting a `Thread` is not really necessary and introduces a significant overhead.

I would love to see if we can find a way to make both strategies to work :)

The outer `try/except` block is raising a `KeyError`, because Enums are indexed by *strings*, but they iterate their (object) members:
```pycon
>>> for prop in MyEnum:
...     MyEnum[prop]
...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 2, in <module>
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/enum.py", line 327, in __getitem__
    return cls._member_map_[name]
KeyError: <MyEnum.MY_CONSTANT: functools.partial(<function...>)>
```

The first argument to the KeyError is the invalid key, which was `MyEnum.MY_CONSTANT`. When you pickle the exception, you pickle that argument. 

But that object itself can't be (un)pickled; that's the source of the ValueError (not tblib):
```pycon
>>> import pickle
>>> pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(MyEnum.MY_CONSTANT))
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/enum.py", line 291, in __call__
    return cls.__new__(cls, value)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/enum.py", line 533, in __new__
    return cls._missing_(value)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/enum.py", line 546, in _missing_
    raise ValueError("%r is not a valid %s" % (value, cls.__name__))
ValueError: functools.partial(<function...>) is not a valid MyEnum
```

I don't know the rules for pickling/unpickling `Enum` instances, but this code seems to break them :)

The `_get_shared_string_index()` method in SharedStrings isn't thread safe. The other methods in that class should be since they are only called from Workbook (for which there should only be one unique instance per xlsxwriter object) or via the Workbook destructor (when the worksheet threads should be `joined` and activity stopped).

> i've discovered that if i use in_memory option XlsxWriter will use SharedStrings

The SharedStrings class can be called regardless of whether you use `in_memory` or not. The `in_memory` option relates to the use of temporary files and not SharedStrings ([see the docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/workbook.html#constructor)).

If you wish to avoid using SharedStrings, and thus avoid locking, you can use the `constant_memory` mode. There are some caveats to that though: data needs to be written in row-column order and you can't use `add_table()` or `merge_range()`. See the Contructor docs (link above) and also [Working with Memory and Performance](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/working_with_memory.html#memory-perf).

> And is there anything else in XlsxWriter that i need to worry about?

Possibly, but nothing that springs to mind.

To me it makes sense that pypi doesn't allow you to re-upload a faulty package because you would be re-using the same version number for two different software releases. You see this pattern in most hosted software platforms: from CDNs to Linux distro packaging. The link you posted does actually have a solution for this particular problem but I guess you'd have to bump the minor version number (e.g. 1.6.1 or something).

We all really appreciate that you shared this very useful piece of software. ``` Python
from chatterbot.filters import Filter

class MyTestFilter(Filter):

    def filter_selection(self, chatterbot, session_id):
        session = chatterbot.conversation_sessions.get(session_id)

        if session.conversation.empty():
            return chatterbot.storage.base_query

        text_of_recent_responses = []

        for statement, response in session.conversation:
            text_of_recent_responses.append(response.text)

        query = chatterbot.storage.base_query.statement_text_not_in(
            text_of_recent_responses
        )

        return query

I think we should catch ChecksumErrors here and wrap them around `ChecksumError(DeviceException)`. Something like:
```
try:
    m = Message.parse(data, ctx)

    raise ChecksumError("Invalid checksum") from ex

After giving this a good bit of thought, I'm leaning toward 4 above: drop the post-create step in `replace()`. The new design woudl be that `replace()` will just create the new object by calling the class's `__init__()` with the right params (which will call `__post_init__()` as usual), and then return that object. Also, I think it's correct to drop the current requirement that `init=False` fields have a `default` or `default_factory`. This is the approach that `attrs` takes.

My reasoning is that it's not dataclass's job to make sure that the created object has all of the fields set, it's the class author's job. If it's possible that a code path through `__init__` and `__post_init__` leaves a field unset, that's a bug in the class definition. Or maybe it's not a bug, depending on the requirements for that class. Trying to guess the author's intent is just not feasible. Consenting adults and all.

After all, if the class has some wacky creation rules that might leave some fields unset, then that's what `__post_init__` is for: you can have your own post-creation verification and/or additional field setting logic.

This sounds reasonable. Although I still like my original proposal of using `copy.copy()` by default (but only when the argument is not specified), and maybe optimizing that out if it's a common immutable type (e.g. bool, int, float, complex, str, bytes, tuple, frozenset).

I've helped @untech today who stubled upon a similar problem. He used `multiprocessing.imap_unordered` to iterate through texts, parse them with `mwparserfromhell.parse` in parallel, and do other transformations. He got the following traceback:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 914, in _bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 862, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/multiprocessing/pool.py", line 429, in _handle_results
    task = get()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/multiprocessing/connection.py", line 253, in recv
    return ForkingPickler.loads(buf.getbuffer())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/string_mixin.py", line 111, in __getattr__
    return getattr(self.__unicode__(), attr)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/nodes/text.py", line 38, in __unicode__
    return self.value
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/nodes/text.py", line 49, in value
    return self._value

  [...]
  
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/string_mixin.py", line 111, in __getattr__
    return getattr(self.__unicode__(), attr)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/nodes/text.py", line 38, in __unicode__
    return self.value
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/mwparserfromhell/nodes/text.py", line 49, in value
    return self._value
RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
```

Eventually, it turned out that there were incorrect texts, and `parse` raised `ValueError` on them. This exception contained some information about source texts (in particular, StringMixin subclasses).

Since processes don't share common address space, Python uses pickle to send information between them. This includes sending of occured exceptions, but StringMixin inside the ValueErrors failed to be deserialized with pickle due to a bug with infinite calls in __getattr__. This led to the very unclear traceback above.

One way to fix this problem in the library can be to make subclasses of StringMixin pickle-serializable. If this isn't possible, I think this post would be useful to people who stumbled upon the same problem anyway. The possible solution for them is to handle exceptions from mwparserfromhell inside the child process and return a serializable error indicator.

The plugin approach works for my use case : a simple directive to use on the same element stTable is defined upon, that reloads (using ctrl.slice) the table.

The directive/plugin code can be found [here](https://github.com/matgardon/SmartTable-external-data-context/blob/master/README.md) for anyone to use (I don't have time to write unit tests, but could this be interesting to commit to the main repo as a complement to stPipe ?).

Main problems left with this approach :
* The plugin/directive can be used weather the data is externally loaded or not : that is not good as in case of internally managed data, the expected behavior is to reload the data-soure, which will not be the case.
* Due to the above problem, the behavior of smart-table is quite difficult to apprehend between externally and internally managed data : in the first case, one has to provide a way to load data to smart-table & manage every external event accordingly with plugins, in the other, one can manage the collection smart-table is bound to, and updates to this collection will be reflected in smart-table as expected. The 2 behaviors are quite different for developers and not that well explained in the docs.

You're using `unicodecsv` as a drop-in replacement for `csv`. The problem is that it's only a reasonable drop-in replacement for _Python 2_ `csv`. If it attempted to be a drop-in replacement for Python 3's as well, it would be a different API, as was already demonstrated. I don't see a reasonable way to make it be both compatible between Python 2 and 3 _and_ be a drop-in replacement for both versions. That's because they have radically different semantics.

The new Python 3 work doesn't add Python 2's semantics to Python 3, but rather adds Python 3's _clarity of purpose_ to the API presented on Python 2. The Python 2 API was the invariant that couldn't be changed, I just had to codify what that API was doing using Python 3's strict split between binary and text.

I'd rather be explicit about how we expect this module to be used.  The -cliff bug is one of implicit expectation that happened to work before.  

At the time I initially wrote the module, py3 may have existed but was very early in terms of community usage.  I really just meant it to make unicode CSV processing easier in py2.  Obviously the world has moved on, and I think py3's approach to encoding is clean and reasonable.  It's *different*, so it does require some learning and adaptation.  I can see that this library could continue to be a useful shim in dual-support for 2/3, but I would also hope that unless the intention is to continue supporting 2/3 from a single codebase, people would then migrate to native py3 csv.

With that in mind, I'd prefer to document:

1) how to use unicodecsv as a 2/3 shim (noting expectations of stream details and at which layer encoding is handled).
2) how to migrate code which uses unicodecsv to using py3's native support for unicode in CSVs.

The 0.14.1 change is a breaking change for what I'd consider edge cases, and I'd say we should either note that people preferring the old way should stick to 0.13.0, or update their code to be more explicit.

Please, read the [docs](http://python-redmine.readthedocs.io/operations.html#filter) before using the library to understand the concepts:

> ResourceSet object is lazy, i.e. it doesn’t make any requests to Redmine when it is created and is evaluated only when some of these conditions are met:

> Iteration. A ResourceSet is iterable and it is evaluated when you iterate over it.
> for project in redmine.project.all():
        print(project.name)

> len(). A ResourceSet is evaluated when you call len() on it and returns the length of the list.
> len(redmine.project.all())

> list(). Force evaluation of a ResourceSet by calling list() on it.
> list(redmine.project.all())

> Index. A ResourceSet is also evaluated when you request some of it’s Resources by index.
> redmine.project.all()[0]  # Returns the first Resource in the ResourceSet

> I don't think is visibly mentioned in the docs

I tried to document it in the docstring of `USBContext.open`, although I did it after discovering and understanding the issue so it may not be the best way to describe or explain it. Suggestions very welcome.

> I can't easily restructure my code to look like you suggest.

I know, I was very much annoyed when I was told unordered garbage collection is just how it works and not a bug, and that context managers were the only reliable solution (and their `try..finally: close()` equivalent).

There may be an alternative solution in rewriting python-libusb1 in C so that it can control garbage collection one level below the interpreter. But I have no intention of doing so, as then it would likely not work with pypy.

As an alternative to context management, note that you can also call `<context>.close()` before your program exits (so preferably in a `try..finally` block).

> Patch updated.

You need to attach an instance of c_void_p to the class, again because of interpreter shutdown issues: module globals are deleted (replaced by `None`) before all instances in that module have been destroyed. This is why all (otherwise) globals which are involved in a destructor (and its callees) are all bound to classes, as `__null_pointer` was, despite how redundant and uggly it looks. Otherwise, on interpreter shutdown one will see `None` values popping here and there (here it would be `NoneType is not callable`).

I don't object to offering decorators since they ensure there's only one "right" way to get the job done.  However, I'd like point out that there's a case against both of the proposed behaviors (parent existence, parent permissions):

 - Enforcing parent permissions by default would prohibit a POST to `facebook.com/post/{id}/comments`.  I imagine there are many other examples of this kind of behavior including `like`, `subscribe` and `attend`.
 - I have a serializer that creates a related object if it does not exist.  The full representation of that related object is included in the payload.  It's counter-intuitive, but there's no logical reason why the related object could be the "parent" in the URL structure.

I think the first bullet is strong enough to argue against permissions checking by default.  The second bullet is weak enough that I'm +1 on existence checking by default.

Hi, if I'm not mistaken, the problem you are facing is that in the first three lines such as:

```python
r = types.InlineQueryResultArticle('1', 'Bold', reply_markup=markup, types.InputTextMessageContent("*{}*".format(query.query), parse_mode="Markdown"))
```

You are specifying `input_message_content` **after** `reply_markup`. In Python, arguments (mandatory and without a default value) must be placed before keyword arguments (optional and/or with a default value), which can appear in any order as long as they are identified. I think the solution would be as simple as to invert the ordering:

```python
r = types.InlineQueryResultArticle('1', 'Bold', types.InputTextMessageContent("*{}*".format(query.query), parse_mode="Markdown"), reply_markup=markup)
```

Users created by django-python3-ldap are created with no privileges  and no is_staff flag, so won't be able to log into the admin system.

You can customize user model creation by overriding the LDAP_AUTH_CLEAN_USER_DATA and LDAP_AUTH_SYNC_USER_RELATIONS settings.

https://github.com/etianen/django-python3-ldap#available-settings

For example:

``` py
# settings.py
LDAP_AUTH_CLEAN_USER_DATA = "your_package.ldap.custom_clean_user_data

# your_package.ldap
from django_python3_ldap.utils import clean_user_data

def custom_clean_user_data(user_data):
    user_data = clean_user_data(user_data)
    user_data.is_staff = True
    return user_data
```

Whenever you type "plot" in the notebook, what actually happens under the hood is approximately the following:

    gcf().gca().plot(...)

Where `gcf()` is "get current figure" and `gca()` means "get current axis". If you type `plot` at the time when no figure was previously created, matplotlib initializes it for you with some defaults and creates a default axis on it. If you do not like the defaults you can redefine them later:

    gcf().set_figheight(10)
    gcf().set_figwidth(10)

You can also take figure creation in your own hands and call `figure(figsize=(10, 10))` before plotting anything - the `gcf()` function will then refer to that exact figure you just created. 

To summarize. Either do (the usual approach)

    figure(figsize=(10, 10))
    venn3(...)

or (less usual but still valid approach)

    venn3(...)
    gcf().set_figheight(10)
    ...

I don't remember why (that was one year ago), but i faced the same pb at beginning and i resolved such a lazy init by using blueprints (check the sample/blueprints.py file) instead of classic app and api.init_app.

Bind your api to a blueprint in a global way:
```python
# Flask API handler for this particular API package version
current_blueprint = Blueprint(__name__, __name__)
# noinspection PyTypeChecker
api = Api(app=current_blueprint)
```
Decorate your api with the swagger docs method (you can do it at runtime on a submethod to allow to control what you expose or not according to a config file, instead of a globally way as shown in samples) and add resources endpoints to your api:
```python
def my_init(flask_app):
    api = swagger.docs(
        api,
        apiVersion="v1.2.3",
        basePath=url_prefix,
        api_spec_url="/api-docs",
        description="My Too Hype API"),
    )

    # Add a resource API to show on your web interface
    api.add_resource(my_pkg.MyServiceView,
                     '/my_pkg',
                     endpoint="{}.{}".format(__name__, "my_pkg.MyServiceView"))

    # Bind your blueprint to you flask_app arg
    flask_app.register_blueprint(current_blueprint, url_prefix=url_prefix)

```

Then bind you app to the blueprint in a lazy way at runtime, in a (main/wsgi)-like method:
````python
def main():
    app = Flask("xxx", static_folder='../static')
    my_init(app)
```

> the fontTools pen will include the glyph's anchors when calculating bounds

yes, if one uses the fonttools pen to draw a whole glyph (like in @typemytype example above). If one uses the `draw` method of a contour, anchors are not passed on to the pen (they have their own `draw` and `drawPoints` methods).

> the _get_bounds is on BooleanContour (not Glyph) so anchors shouldn't get drawn in the first place here

exactly. For this particular instance, the only difference between using the fonttools' or robofab's BoundsPen is that the latter excludes single-point paths.

From the point of view of booleanOperations, one is only interested in the bounds of **closed** paths, and a single-point contour is open by definition, though it's not the only one. Maybe a more appropriate bounds pen for booleanOperations is one that excludes not only single points but any non-closed paths?

Hi Frederik;

The problem actually isn't about closing paths at all. Open paths are already handled by the code quite well. The problem is that in some cases, Robofont adds an unnecessary initial 'move' to an outline.The current code  triggers an assert that a 'move' cannot be in the path , at line 341 in flatten.py:addPoint().  If you use  flatten.py::.ContourPointDataPen to get the data, then the initial move is deleted in flatten.ContourPointDataPen.getData()  before flatten.py:OutputContour.drawPoints. However, booleanGlypy.py uses robofab.pens.adapterPens.SegmentToPointPen, which does not remove the point. It is clearly a good idea to remove the unnecessary initial move pt, the question is where. I am reluctant to edit robofab.pens.adapterPens.SegmentToPointPen, so it  makes more sense to me to remove it in booleanGlyph. It probably would be cleaner to subclass SegmentToPointPen and add the cleanup code there.

As @tomblench said "Documents are locally cached as a feature".
Calling `save()` updates the remote db and then updates the local dict copy of the document with the new `_rev`. That is the intended behaviour and the behaviour I am able to reproduce locally both with or without a context manager.
The `db[document_id]` will `fetch()` if the document is not present in the local dict, but will not update the dict if there is already a local copy.

Thus the behaviour I would expect for your scenario (and what I see locally) is:
1. Call update
    1. Remote call to check for DB
    1. Add DB to local dict
    1. Remote call to fetch document
    1. Add document to local dict (rev 1-xxxx)
    1. Document content updated
    1. Remote call to update document (makes rev 2-xxxx)
    1. Local dict `_rev` updated (now rev 2-xxxx)
1. Call get
    1. Local dict DB is returned
    1. Local dict document is returned (rev 2-xxxx)
1. Call update
    1. Local dict DB is returned
    1. Local dict document is returned (rev 2-xxxx)
    1. Document content updated
    1. Remote call to update document (makes rev 3-xxxx)
    1. Local dict `_rev updated` (now rev 3-xxxx)
1. Call get
    1. Local dict DB is returned
    1. Local dict document is returned (rev 3-xxxx)

Given that you don't see that behaviour my guess is that your serverless env is re-using the container for some calls and using a new container for others. For example the behaviour you see could be caused by the client/dict combination used to do the first `update` or first `get` being re-used for the second `get` (i.e. with the second `update` happening in a different container instance and so the local update to `_rev 3-xxxx` happens in a completely different dict instance). IIUC [AWS Lambda, for example,](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/container-reuse-in-lambda/) is at liberty to re-use containers. I'm not sure how deep that re-use goes and what level of re-initialization of objects etc happens, but that's an environment thing that we wouldn't debug.

Doing an explicit fetch (or having the context manager perform that automatically) ensures the state of the local dict is "current" in whatever container you're in before you get the document from it. There is currently no way to disable the local caching in python-cloudant.

This potential race condition is very difficult to produce. It may happen if the output of an MKLDNN operator is used with a sparse array in an operator. However, the current implementation of sparse operators doesn't support this case and fall back using FCompute.

The issue turned out to be that both scaling and normalization are needed to match the pytorch transforms.

```
def transformer(data, label):
    data = mx.image.imresize(data, 256, 256)
    data, _ = mx.image.center_crop(data, (224, 224))
    data = data.astype(np.float32)
    data = data/255
    data = mx.image.color_normalize(data,

To provide a bit of a background on this ticket:

MySqlToGCS operator has a default mapping of mysql field_type to BigQuery type, specified in the `type_map` classmethod in this operator. 

Unfortunately, with mysqldb client in PY2, it turns out to be very difficult to discern between VARBINARY and VARCHAR, they will both return as FIELD_TYPE.VARCHAR in mysqldb client (similarly, between CHAR/BINARY, BLOB/TEXT), so in the past, all binary fields in mysql were mapped to String. There is a binary flag returned by the cursor,  which we originally attempted to use to discern the types. However if binary collation is on, then the binary flag will be set to true regardless of the field type. 

The work-around is to allow user to provide custom `schema` as an operator argument, so that it can override the default mapping. And if it's specified as a binary field, it will be base64 encoded, since binary types in BQ is always base64 encoded.

@d-lee  @jlowin sorry about the confusion, I used .iteritems() out of habits, but definitely agree .items() is the way to go.

As for the question about why encoding was used,  'bytes' type was only introduced in Python3, so .encode() in python2 encodes 'unicode' to 'str'. 

In particular, this code is probably encoding 'str' to 'str' for the most part, and it works for a very evil reason. It sees that the object is not a unicode and first try to implicitly coerce it into unicode (which works since it's ascii by default) and then encode it into string... I think the reason I did this was because I was hitting unicode errors during testing for some reason with data that were passed in, but I cannot recall exactly.

I do agree that for this to be python3 compatible we should not be encoding it. As long as removing the encode will still work with 2, Im more than happy for the change! Thanks so much for the fix.

I think that last sentence is the actual key: all of the default formats except `byte` have the same potential behavior, if they get input that isn't pre-validated, but testing invalid inputs for those (using GET requests to a pyramid_swagger-enabled app), I get JSON schema validation errors for invalid integer and number formats.  So, it looks like formatter is expecting the schema validation to have caught the invalid date-time format.

Looking at [jsonschema._format](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/master/jsonschema/_format.py#L172), it seems to have validation if and only if `strict_rfc3339` or `isodate` is available, which is [optional](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/master/setup.py#L24). So, installing [strict-rfc3339](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/strict-rfc3339) should make validation fail in a more predictable way (pause...) and it does!  And removing it and installing [isodate](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/isodate) also does.

So, my originally proposed patch is not the right way to go: it seems like adding the right dependency so that jsonschema validates dates correctly is the better approach.  I think that means just changing the [dependency](../blob/master/setup.py#L33) to `jsonschema[format]>=2.5.1`—does that seem sane?

@brandonhall It's hard to say without knowing the exact code and server response. If you remove Migrate and your DELETE requests don't throw errors, then you wouldn't need Migrate for this situation. If the server correctly returned `Content-type: application/json` for 200 JSON responses and either no type or something like `Content-type: text/plain` for 204 empty responses, then you could just remove the `datatype:  "json"` from the call. Or the server can continue to return a JSON content type and just return a valid JSON response such as `{}` instead of nothing.

The problem with the case @hitsthings reported is that the contract `$.ajax()` gives the user says the first argument of the `success` callback is "the data returned from the server, formatted according to the dataType parameter ." It does not go on to say, "If the server sometimes gives you some other content type, or no content at all, we'll  return that as the first argument even though we can't convert it to the data type you requested." As you can imagine that leads to really hard-to-find bugs, which is why this behavior was changed in 1.9.

I think if I had my druthers, I'd have two things:

1. A mock for this library that let developers test authentication flows so they can write tests for their site that cover "what happens if the user doesn't exist?", "what happens if the user exists, but is disabled?", "what happens if the user claim data looks like this?", etc. Testing authentication flows is a pain in the ass. 48679e4 is a pretty drastic change, but it solves the problem of associating arbitrary notifiers with tags. Given that, I need to think on it to be sure it isn't fundamentally flawed, but feedback would be appreciated @SylvainCorlay, @minrk, @ellisonbg.

The gist of it:
+ All notifiers are wrapped in EvenHandlers
+ The `_trait_notifiers` and `_trait_validators` `HasTraits` attributes are lists instead of dicts.
+ Collecting callbacks is done using the `names` and `tags` attrs on `EventHandlers` and requires this to be computed whenever `_notify_change` is called.

I have a feeling that this will slow down how quickly notifiers are called since collecting them isn't as easy as accessing them via dictionary.

os.environ.setdefault("DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "mysite.settings")
from django.conf imprt settings

@anarcat Now that I had a look at it, I think we should return the revocation keys as a list. I think we should not try to deduplicate the revocation key subpackets, as PGPy can come across a key that has multiple identical revocation key subpackets, which might not make sense, but I think we should not remove them here.

Also, it is not such a simple change to a set, as the `RevocationKey` namedtuple contains a list as an attribute (from the `RevocationKey` subpacket), thus is not hashable and cannot be inside a set, so that will need to change to a set as well to actually work.

@oysnow This error can happen sometimes if the server takes a little longer to send a response that we allow time for, or if it takes a little longer for the service's response to make it's way back to the caller (i.e network issues as you suggested).

For example, we establish a connection to EC2 and send a request for spot price history.  We then try to `.read()` a response from EC2.  Meanwhile EC2 is working on generating the response to send back to us but hasn't sent anything yet.  I believe our default timeout is 60 seconds.  If after 60 seconds EC2 still hasn't sent us a response we time out and propagate the exception back to the caller.

I think a good addition for us is to add config options for setting the `.read()` timeout on connections so that users that run into situations like this can increase this timeout.

The other option is that we could retry this, but given this is because the service hasn't responded quickly enough, retrying has potential to make this problem worse.

Let me know if you have any other questions.  I've filed https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/192 to track the read timeout configuration option.

Here's a summary of where the session caching functionality in `botocore` is at

* The session caching in this PR was merged into `botocore` via #1338 and made available in [`botocore` 1.8.14](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/CHANGELOG.rst#1814).
* So as to not interfere with `awscli`, the default on disk location of the cache was set to [`~/.aws/boto/cache`](https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/1338/files#diff-dcd72ae45553af71f1ceafb2d4ee5dcfR192). This means that, by default, cached credentials are not shared between `awscli` and `botocore`.
* The ability to enable session caching in botocore via a [config setting](https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/1157/commits/74a061e87141c7f4615068461cad4b881ddff769) was not included in #1338 and was not merged into `botocore`. I'm looking for more guidance on when or how we'd be able to get this configuration setting functionality in #1461.

In order to use this new session caching functionality, you'll need to both
* enable it in your code instead of your user's config
* set the `working_dir` where the on disk cache files are stored to be the same as where `awscli` stores it's cache. This is optional. With it `awscli` and `botocore` share a single set of cached sessions reducing the frequency with which you have to type your MFA code.

Here's a [code snippet](https://github.com/boto/botocore/pull/1338#issuecomment-368472031) from @mixja showing how to use the new functionality

```
from botocore import credentials
import botocore.session
import boto3
import os

# Change the cache path from the default of ~/.aws/boto/cache to the one used by awscli
working_dir = os.path.join(os.path.expanduser('~'),'.aws/cli/cache')

# Construct botocore session with cache
session = botocore.session.get_session()
provider = session.get_component('credential_provider').get_provider('assume-role')
provider.cache = credentials.JSONFileCache(working_dir)

# Create boto3 client from session
client = boto3.Session(botocore_session=session).client('s3')

print(client.list_buckets())
```
Looks good to me.  I think I'd still like to also see a functional test that uses a real client via `session.create_client(...)` to ensure everything's hooked up properly.

That's true only on Python 3 though. What probably shaped my thinking a bit is that most of my day to day work is still Python 2 where `str()` returns raw bytes, not text.

However, I agree that it might be best to put Python 3 idioms ahead of Python 2 and I'm happy to axe `__str__` in that light. Doing that probably also diminishes the value of having a Python 3-specific `__bytes__`.

AFAICT, `Blob` implements the buffer protocol, but `bytes(blob)` only works on Python 3. On Python 2 it relies on `Blob.__str__` which doesn't return the blob's contents. The only 2/3-compatible way to access a blob's bytes seems to be through `Blob.data`, or something like `memoryview(blob).tobytes()` through the buffer protocol interface directly.

What would you think of carrying Blob's interface forward and adding `Patch.data` instead of `as_bytes()`? Additionally I could also implement the buffer protocol onto Patch, so that it behaves identically to Blob.

Using the `tag.object` it should be straightforward to obtain the raw-bytes stored in the tag-object, in case this is what you are actually looking for. Those represent a few formatted lines of information, which could be parsed with code similar to the one [currently in use](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/tag.py#L55).
Parsing can only safely operate on bytes though, as the encoding seems not to be UTF-8 at all times.

Even if parsing is made to work at some point, right now the tagger-name are expected to be `str/unicode` instances, which couldn't be obtained if the encoding of the underlying bytes are unknown.
I think this is fixed now: https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/902679c47c3d1238833ac9c9fdbc7c0ddbedf509/git/remote.py#L553 .

What you have been doing was equivalent to `git branch master`, which fails with `fatal: Not a valid object name: 'master'.` on the commandline as well.
However, I have [added a new test-case](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/test/test_repo.py#L690) to verify gitpython deals with empty repositories gracefully.
In addition to that, the BadObject exception is now a `BadName` exception, which doesn't convert perfectly nice names to some hex, which would just add to the confusion.

Unfortunately, this issue cannot be fixed on the side of GitPython. Back in the days, I wrote the code as if there were deterministic destructors, which seemed to be the case in py2.7. However, since py3, this clearly is not the case at all anymore, and one simply shouldn't use `__del__` at all.

Code involving the writer now has to be written like so:

```python
writer = repo.config_writer()
writer.set_value('section', 'key', 'value')
writer.release()
```

If that is not done, bad things can happen, even though I wouldn't know why it can also lead to a wiped configuration file as you describe it.

As noted, you will have to change all code involving a `config_writer()` to release it after use - you will find this kind of code throughout the GitPython code-base as well.

Please let me know if this makes sense to you.

This isn't a big deal, but in my opinion model objects like `CookieInformation` should explicitly list arguments they expect rather than getting in some other data that they need to handle somehow. Model objects being independent on certain external data presentation makes them easier to use in different context and to unit test without mocking/stubbing. Arguments accepted by model objects should, when feasible, also have default values to make using them even more flexible. This I'd prefer code like this:

```python
class CookieInformation(object):

    def __init__(self, name, value, expiry=None, ...):
        self.name = name
        self.value = value
        self.expiry = # code to handle expiry somehow
```

This could then be used, for example, like:

```python
cookie = CookieInformation(data['name'], data['value'], data.get('expiry'), ...)
```

Also, if we add separate `full_info` property for logging purposes, it should return a string.

Requirements in this case (or for `install_requires`/`setup_requires`):
* a single requirement as a string
* a series of requirements as a list
* a series of requirements as a multi-line strings

```python
{'im_an_extra_dependency': 'thing'}
{'im_an_extra_dependency': ['thing']}
{'im_an_extra_dependency': '''
thing
'''}
```

**Try changing the matplotlib backend to get matplotlib to work in a virtual environment:**

There are (at least) two solutions I've used, with some additional methods included below. Both outlined here should to the same thing with a slightly different approach:

If you already installed matplotlib using pip on your virtual environment, you can...

_1. Add a new matplotlibrc file for your virtual environment_
For example, in your virtual environment run:
```
$ cd ~/.matplotlib
$ nano matplotlibrc #to create file using nano
```
Write `backend: TkAgg` in the file and save upon exit. You should be good to go.

****OR****

_2. Edit the matplotlibrc file referenced by your virtual environment_
In your virtual environment, open a python shell and run:
```
>>> import matplotlib
>>> matplotlib.matplotlib_fname()
'/home/foo/.config/matplotlib/matplotlibrc'
```

import matplotlib

The `json_encoder` is used when triggering events to encode the payload of each event as a string, which is then sent in the json body of the request itself. Only this payload is encoded by the `json_encoder`. For example, if we `.trigger("c", "e", { "foo": "bar" })` then the json of the request is

```json
{"name": "e", "channels": ["c"], "data": "{\"foo\": \"bar\"}"}
```

Note that `data` is just a string – this is the only bit that gets touched by the `json_encoder`. The push notification API doesn't have this extra level of encoding, everything is just in the root JSON object. i.e. if we `.trigger(["i"], { "foo": "bar" })` the body of the response is just

```json
{"interests": ["i"], "foo": "bar"}
```

There's no double encoding.

That being said, what is your use case for using the `json_encoder` for the notify? It certainly might be worth us looking at utilising it regardless.

```
"""
Word count topology
"""

from streamparse import Grouping, Topology, Stream

from bolts.wordcount import WordCountBolt
from spouts.words import WordSpout


class WordCount(Topology):
    class XWordSpout(WordSpout):
        outputs = [
            Stream(fields=['word'],
                   name='first',),
            Stream(fields=['word'],
                   name='second',),
        ]

    word_spout = XWordSpout.spec()
    first_count_bolt = WordCountBolt.spec(name=b"wordcount_first", inputs={word_spout['first']: Grouping.SHUFFLE},
                                    par=2)
    second_count_bolt = WordCountBolt.spec(name=b"wordcount_second", inputs={word_spout['second']: Grouping.SHUFFLE},

I've never used `iraf.phot` and I doubt any other `photutils` developers have either.  The parameters for `photutils` aperture photometry are very straightforward.  To define a circular aperture the only parameters are `x`, `y`, and `r` (radius).  For the `aperture_photometry` function, the `mask` and `method` (and `subpixels`) keywords are the only parameters that could change the photometry results.  Note that the default is `method=exact`, which may not be same method your tool uses.  For example, to get the exact results as `SExtractor` (a standard photometry tool) one would use `method='subpixel', subpixels=5` (`photutils` has been tested against `SExtractor`).

Note that `iraf.phot` may also be changing your input data (e.g. global/local background subtraction and/or masking of bad data values) or parameters (e.g. re-centroiding `x, y`), which would also lead to differences with `photutils` (I suspect this may be the case). `photutils.aperture_photometry` does not subtract any background nor does it re-centroid `x, y` (but there are other tools in `photutils` to do both).  If you want any data values to be masked, you can input a `mask` array to `aperture_photometry`.

You could try both tools on a simulated source where you know the correct answer to see what is different.  `photutils` has been well-tested in this regard (and against `SExtractor`), but I can't rule out a possible edge-case bug.

Hey @sciunto,

Canny filtering using the default skimage.feature implementation does not provide exact perimeters, as it first smooths with a Gaussian filter, then morphologically thins edges and performs hystersis. 

This naturally leads to perimeters that are slightly "inside" the original mask. I would assume they'll be 1/2 the size of the Gaussian filter inside in fact, as the maximum values post-filtering will be 1/2 filter width inside the actual positive binary object.

The morphological padding, erosion, unpadding, subtraction algorithm implemented here finds the *actual* edges of objects in the mask.

This is easiest to show with a couple examples.

`from skimage import feature`
`edges = feature.canny(mask)`  
`perim = binary_perimeter(mask) # as implemented in this PR`
`imgplt = imshowpair(mask, edges)`
`plt.show()`

One last thought for now--it looks like I might still be able to do *something* by invoking the Windows API directly to register [vectored continue handler](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms679273(v=vs.85).aspx).  This is in fact how Cygwin itself handles exceptions that occur while on the alternate stack.  Unfortunately Cygwin's handler *only* handles exceptions that occur from within Cygwin (which is the only context where it knows how to safely unwind the stack).  Exceptions that come from outside Cygwin, as in this case, are just given up on and the process is allowed to run off into the weeds (actually Windows just kills it).  But Cysignals provides an opportunity to still do something a little better here I think.

[Here](https://github.com/vinklein/cysignals/wiki/How-to-install-cysignals-on-Windows) is a first step-by-step guide to install cysignals on windows in case if someone want to test the current [windows_dev](https://github.com/vinklein/cysignals/commits/windows_dev).

@lnielsen To summarize, the signature of Admin's ModelView looks like this:

http://flask-admin.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/mod_contrib_sqla/#flask_admin.contrib.sqla.ModelView

So ModelView and the Model are actually ``args`` where the categorization and other custom view options are ``kwargs``. I see two good ways of defining an entry point - using a 3-tuple:

```Python
ep_item = (ModelView,
           Model,
           dict(category="CategoryName",
                menu_class_name="MenuClassName"),
)
```
or using a single dict:
```Python
ep_item = dict(modelview=ModelView,
               model=Model,
               category="CategoryName",
               menu_class_name="MenuClassName", ...)
```

The first one has quite explicit resolution in admin's init:

```Python
                modelview, model, kwargs = ep.load()

                # Add default security to the model view
                ProtectedAdminView = protected_adminview_factory(modelview)
                self.admin.add_view(ProtectedAdminView(model, db.session,
                    *kwargs))

```

The second requires some manual pulling of 'model' and 'modelview' from the dict and passing the rest as ``kwargs``.

I'm assuming the latter (single dict object in EP) is better ?

I think it's also worth to include in the documentation brief definition of the concepts. Where those are defined in Flask Principal, or other library it would be great if a proper reference is made. So that the user can start using `invenio-access` without knowing it's dependencies.

Hi @nigelsmith! Thank you for your comment!

Yep, you are right - `initial_data` are unvalidated and they shouldn't be used here. Furthermore, this is internal serializer API. Unfortunately, there is no easy way to access validated data in `save` or `save_object` method (DRF2). As you pointed out, this problem is fixed in DRF3. One of reasons why DRF author changed it, fit very well into `djoser` issue - DRF3 changelog:

> Instantiating model instances directly means that you cannot use model manager classes for instance creation, e.g. `ExampleModel.objects.create(...)`. Manager classes are an excellent layer at which to enforce business logic and application-level data constraints.

If we still want to use `User.objects.create_user` (which is good idea IMHO) we could call it in `restore_object` method:

```python
def restore_object(self, attrs, instance=None):
    return User.objects.create_user(**attrs)
```

... but in this case we also have to override `save_object` method:

```python
def save_object(self, obj, **kwargs):
    return obj
```

... because it uses `obj.save(force_insert=True)`, which will raise `IntegrityError` in this case (user was created in `restore_object`). But even if we choose this option (which I don't like), there will be problem with DRF3:

> AssertionError: Serializer `djoser.serializers.UserRegistrationSerializer` has old-style version 2 `.restore_object()` that is no longer compatible with REST framework 3. Use the new-style `.create()` and `.update()` methods instead.

Maybe we should create different classes for DRF2 and DRF3 (we could check `rest_framework.VERSION`)? We could also move creation step into view `create` method, but this will be also problematic - DRF3 has different flow in this method.

@FrancescAlted: Thanks for the quick fix. **Unfortunately, the code above is still broken ** And thus pytables read_where() queries using VML functions is still broken.

9169600 fixes ```numexpr.evaluate('log10(...)'``` but the script above (and pytables.read_where() use ```NumExpr.run()```. The patched code in 9169600 is then bypassed and a VML function is run on non-contiguous memory.

This affects all queries using functions in pytables.read_where() when VML is used. I tested with sqrt() and log10() and both are broken. 

New self-contained test-script, based on script above:
```
"""
Simulate a read_where() query on a pytables table
"""
import numpy
import numexpr
from numexpr.necompiler import NumExpr, stringToExpression


def make_NumExpr(expr):
    """ Make a NumExpr object exactly as pytables """

    mysig = [('f1', float)]
    typemap = {'f1': float}
    strexpr = stringToExpression(expr, typemap, {})
    print "DEBUG: strexpr ", strexpr

    return NumExpr(strexpr, mysig)

def make_recarray():
    """ make a pytables table-like numpy.rec.array """

    data = numpy.rec.array(None, formats=('f4,f4,f4'), shape=(5,))
    data['f0']= numpy.arange(5)
    data['f1']= numpy.arange(200,205)
    data['f2']= numpy.arange(5)

    return data

if __name__ == '__main__':
    print "numexpr version: ", numexpr.__version__
    print "VML: ", numexpr.get_vml_version()

    expr_log10 = make_NumExpr('log10(f1)')
    expr_sqrt = make_NumExpr('sqrt(f1)')
    table = make_recarray()

    print "log10() = (2.0x, 2.0x, ..) ", expr_log10(table['f1'])
    print "sqrt() = (14.1, 14.1, ...)", expr_sqrt(table['f1'])

```


Output of the script above:
```
numexpr version:  2.5.2.dev0
VML:  Intel(R) Math Kernel Library Version 11.3.1 Product Build 20151021 for Intel(R) 64 architecture applications
DEBUG: strexpr  FuncNode(log10, float, (VariableNode(f1, float, ()),))
DEBUG: strexpr  FuncNode(sqrt, float, (VariableNode(f1, float, ()),))
log10() = (2.0x, 2.0x, ..)  [ 2.30102992        -inf  0.          2.30319595  0.        ]
sqrt() = (14.1, 14.1, ...) [ 14.14213562   0.           1.          14.17744732   1.        ]

```

> I think the former already fits in well with the current paradigm of returning a dict. 

Not really, unless I am missing something.  Right now, we get a single ChromaticOpticalPSF for a given SCA (so the dict is indexed by SCA).  Then users can use it to draw into whatever band they choose -- and the decisions happens later on.  Once the pupil plane image depends on the wavelength, there would have to be a long- and short-wavelength ChromaticOpticalPSF for a given SCA, and you choose which one to use depending on which band you want to draw into.  This new feature would require specifying the band at the time of calling getPSF(), or the creation of two PSFs for each SCA which makes the output more complicated.

Do you actually care about having the right RA/dec?  You could do a best-guess nearby position for ```world_pos```, find the coordinates that actually live on some SCA (pick your favorite, I guess!), then shift the catalogs RA/dec values appropriately so they live on the SCA.  It seems like this should be fine if you just want to get an image to stare at.  If you want to match it against some other image then that won't work.

In terms of how to better support this use case, I think the way to make it easier for a user to do this would be:
- take the chunk of code in ```getWCS()``` that finds the position on the sky for the center of each SCA given ```world_pos``` and the orientation of the observatory, and turn it into a callable routine that is exposed to the user
- ```getWCS()``` can then call that routine when needed
- users can call that routine in order to back out where SCAs would live given some ```world_pos``` and orientation

Does that sound like it would be helpful?  I could add it to the feature requests in #675 and hopefully address them in the coming weeks.

It seems to be a bug related to a for loop inside a with.
I could reproduce it with the following code:
```python
class ContextManager:

    def __enter__(self):
        return 2

    def __exit__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        pass

def func():
    context = ContextManager()

    with context as value:
        for i in range(value):
            print(i)

func()
```

It occurs because we try to get the calls by lineno and lasti on the bytecode.
WITH_CLEANUP opcode results in a call to __exit__.
By capturing line and lasti of WITH_CLEANUP, we can usually detect the calls. However, in this specific case, the loop mess up with the the lineno, and it fails to obtain the definition of with from the bytecode.
The best way to match the run-time "with" and the bytecode WITH_CLEANUP would be using only the lasti in the code object. 
Since I don't know how to identify the code object in the disassembly, and it would require some time to replace references from lines to code objects, I'll keep the current solution, but silently ignore calls with messed up linenos.
For now, it will be fine. If we improve some collection on with statements (to capture exceptions, for instance), it may be a problem. Hopefully, we will only do that after #84

In case anyone needs a workaround for the getAllowedValues() method, here is my solution.

For custom fields, use pyral v1.1.1 and include the "c_" prefix in the field's name.
For builtin fields, here is a method that specifically retrieves the allowed values of the Milestones field. For other builtin fields, use this method as a beginning template and update accordingly. The method accepts an argument of a Rally object and returns a list of allowed values. Tested with pyral 1.2.0.

def getAllowedMilestonesValues(rally):

    # Get the current Rally context and project reference
    context = rally.contextHelper.currentContext()
    projectRef = rally.contextHelper.currentProjectRef()

    # Construct the url
    resource = ('%s/Milestone?start=1&pagesize=200&fetch=Name&includePermissions=true&compact=true&project=%s'
               '&projectScopeUp=false&projectScopeDown=true' % (context.service_url, projectRef))

    # Call Rally
    response = None
    try:
        response = rally.session.get(resource, timeout=120)
    except Exception as ex:
        print 'Internal Exception: Could not retrieve allowed Milestones values. Status code: %s' % response.status_code
        return None

    if response.status_code != 200:     # HTTP Success code
        print 'Internal Error: Could not retrieve allowed Milestones values. Status code: %s' % response.status_code
        return None

    # Parse the Json response text into a Python data structure
    content = json.loads(response.content)

    # Construct list of Milestone Name strings
    allowedValues = []
    for milestone in content['QueryResult']['Results']:
        allowedValues.append(milestone['Name'])
    return allowedValues

This is a draft.

Following a discussion with @michelbl:

- API example:
  ```python
  class irpp(Variable):
      period_unit = MONTH
  ```
  `period_unit` may be called `periodicity` or `computes_on`
  It would be a required attribute
- `period_unit` is an enum of `{MONTH, YEAR, NO_UNIT}` (`NO_UNIT` may be called `PERMANENT`)
- `new_filled_column` must check its value
- this applies to input variables too
- now a formula can modify its input period in order to call another variable, but the output value must always correspond to the input period
  - as a consequence it is forbidden to use sliding years or months (see https://legislation.openfisca.fr/variables/decote)
- is it possible to call a variable on both periods (month and year)?
- is it possible to compute a formula on instants (and not periods)?
  Is there any formula that uses `period = period.start` or such?
- for the formulas written after the modification involved by this issue, it will be the responsibility of its author to ensure the output result corresponds to the input period. The Core will not be able to check it by itself anymore.  

Implementation related:
- use a cache dict by period unit
- the cache stores values by period as the caller gives it, not by output period anymore
- there is already an attribute [`is_permanent`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/04355b1b570e9df95204d2edc5f890e546116149/openfisca_france/model/caracteristiques_socio_demographiques/demographie.py#L6-L10): we remove it
- in a first stage we can keep the returned `period, result` tuple, to check that the output period equals the input period for all the existing variables
  - then we'll replace it by `return result`

List of special variables:
- `date_de_naissance`
- `nb_enfants_a_charge`
- to be continued

Remind to:
- update the documentation before shipping

This behavior (bug?) is not caused by having shared cookies: as you correctly pointed out, different cookies are used by the browser and the library.

The issues is caused by the JIRA session management code (server side), which logs out all active sessions for a given user if API calls with cookies are made for this very user.

You should be able to reproduce this by:
* Log into JIRA with user `A` in your browser
* Make any API calls, authenticated with the credentials of user `A` by using the library
* Wait 5 minutes
* Try to continue using JIRA in your web browser, you should be logged off.

Note that the logout after 5 minutes still happens even if you continue using JIRA in your browser immediately after the API call.

Please see this as well: https://answers.atlassian.com/questions/11465280/web-user-is-logged-out-when-using-the-rest-api it also provides code snippets to reproduce the issue.

@zssherman I think the way it should be formulated is:

weights = exp(-(dist2) / (2*sigma2)) +1e-5

where sigma2 is the square of sigma, the standard deviation in the Gaussian function. The ROI could then be either specified by the user (but maybe not allowed to be less than sigma) or calculated following Pauley and Wu's (and Barnes') recommendation as:

roi = 2*sqrt(2)*sigma

As for sigma itself, it could again be either specified by the user, or computed as the maximum data spacing (i.e. the beam spacing, in meters, in either azimuth or elevation at the maximum range).

Hi Lucas,

For now it's possible to train a same engine on datasets that have different languages, you just need to load the resources beforehand. However each time you train an engine on a dataset, the engine will be trained for that dataset's language.

In our current setup we will always have one engine per language because the features we use to train the models (specifically the CRF) are highly language specific and yield better than non language specific features/models.

Depending on what you want to achieve you can do the following:
1. Separate your queries in different datasets
2. Train one engine per language
3. Use a language detection library like [langdetect](https://pypi.org/project/langdetect/) to detect the language
2. Use the language engine to perform inference

> I don't see the win for them inside reference docs (which are for refreshing one's memory of how the API works, what parameters it requires/ allows).

The study pointed partly to this (people do want to see what parameters are required) but wanted to see examples of using the method as well. Based on that feedback, I see a big win.

>  they can make the signature of a function/method can be buffers above the actual body, which makes reading the code much harder.

I think relatively few people will read the actual code, far more will read the docs that get generated and live in the gh-pages branch.

---

To be completely clear, I don't see the value in documenting things like `from_api_repr()` with examples (the only people ever using that should be people editing code of gcloud-python). For methods like `list_projects()` I think an example is extremely useful and helps people writing their own code ("Hmm list projects, that sounds right... what's the recommended way to do this...?"), and reading others' code ("Hmm.. this code is using list projects, I wonder if they're doing it 'the right way'?")
