@andwxh
我最近一直在研究怎么给ss-local添加一个acl的功能，基本上快要完成了。目前已经添加了ss-tunnel。ss的整体循环机制我不是很清楚(特别是update_steam)，但是ss的通信过程我还是比较了解的。

关于连接复用的问题，我觉得应该用最简单的方式去实现，http/2实现起来太复杂，对ss来说是彻底的大换血。如果真想用http/2的话用插件去实现不是更好么？

关于udp的问题，现在的ss只是单纯的加密转发，没有任何控制指令，这是现状。如果要添加控制指令的话也不是什么难事吧？至于 tcp over udp，它其实就是一个隧道，里面跑的什么它不需要关心，它需要关心的是怎么把隧道维持好。就像传话筒一样。只是我觉得tcp over udp的方案并不怎么好。

关于上面我提到的那个方案，我想到了一种更好的方法。把 “来源ip和端口”替换成 fd，ss-local接到ss-server发送的数据之后再根据这个fd转发数据。这样只要在原协议的端口字段后面增加一个两字节的字段即可。我已经在尝试这么做了，目前最大的问题是ss的循环机制太复杂了，socks5和local嵌合的太紧了，很难把新功能添加进去。要么大改，要么把它弄得更复杂。我也是在纠结当中。。。不管最终有没有效果，都先把它实现了再说吧。

1985-1989在里根总统时期任美国驻中国大使的温斯顿?洛德最近接受媒体采访时表示：天安门广场的抗议活动将会作为最重要的日子之一载入当代中国史册。

流亡美国的赵紫阳前顾问严家其曾说：“‘六四’是20世纪历史的转折点。如果没有‘六四’，‘柏林墙’就不会在‘六四’后五个月倒塌。在苏联东欧导致了‘一党专政’的崩溃。在中国，导致了共产党政权主动放弃计划经济，并为公有制的瓦解和私人经济的发展敞开大门。”

曾经深入研究过文革历史的旅美中国当代历史学者宋永毅教授在接受BBC中文网采访时这样评价“六四”的历史意义：“‘六四’是中国现当代史上规模最为巨大、影响最为深远的民主运动和学生运动。从1919年的“五四”运动到1989年的“六四”学运的这段中国近现代史中，“六四”无论在规模上还是在影响上都超过了“五四”。

从某种意义上讲，“六四”与“五四”有很多相似之处，都是学生和知识分子带头在北京天安门发起的示威抗议活动。但结局却不相同，北洋政府最初对“五四”学运的反应是镇压逮捕，但最终还是采取了怀柔政策，没有开杀戒；而“六四”天安门学运是以政府出动军队暴力镇压结束的。

宋永毅教授说：“‘五四’运动与‘六四’学运不可比，因为‘五四’也就有上千或几百名学生卷入，在行为方式上也与‘六四’学运不同，‘五四’学运采取了暴力手段，对共产党的成立或国家激进都有负面影响。而整个‘六四’学运今天回头去看完全是非暴力的，而且是井然有序。其追求的目的也非常清楚。”

广州中山大学的历史系教授袁伟时在接受BBC中文网采访时认为，“五四”与“六四”是两个截然不同的事件。他说：“‘五四’是一场对外的爱国民主运动，主要牵涉到维护国家的主权，当时在国内有很强的共识，同时它的结局也是比较圆满的。它提出的一系列主权要求到后来的1920到1921年初的华盛顿会议上基本上都解决了。”

此外，“五四”爱国运动还存有另外一个问题，那就是它的行动是出格的，这包括打人、烧房子、破坏私人财产。当时曾引起一场争议，但大多数人认为只要是爱国，这些都是可以容忍的。

“相比之下，‘六四’学运的很多要求也是有许多共识的，比如反贪腐，要求扩大公民的自由。但‘六四’的问题是学生缺乏政治经验。在好多方面，他们采取的措施不对，结果就引起好多后来的不良后果。”

除此以外，袁伟时教授认为，参加“五四”运动的学生与参与“六四”学运的学生所面对的执政对象也大不相同，一个是中国近代历史上少有的最开明的北洋政府，一个是共产党领导的人民民主专政或无产阶级专政体系。

他说：“北洋时代是三权分立的，总体上来讲有新闻自由、言论自由和结社自由，这在20世纪的中国没有哪个年代可以与之相比。”

宋永毅教授还把“六四”与中共49年建政以来的三次影响巨大的学生运动做了比较，这分别是57年包括北大5?19事件在内的全国校园内出现的学生运动，66年到76年的文革中的红卫兵运动，以及1976年由悼念周总理引发的“4·5”事件。

他说：“57年的学运虽然深度超过了‘六四’，但规模不能比，它主要还是在校园里面。而且很大程度上是共产党引蛇出洞，是共产党为了打击要求民主体制的学生和知识分子而有意导演的一场阳谋，是毛泽东说的阳谋；1966年到1976年的文革中的红卫兵运动虽然在规模上超过‘六四’，但却是反民主的运动。这与‘六四’学运完全不能相比。”

宋永毅教授认为，唯一一个在天安门广场发生的可以称的上是民主运动，而且是以学生为主体的就是1976年的“4?5”事件，也就是所谓的“第一次天安门事件”，但从规模上来讲也就是三千人左右，和“六四”（期间）全中国全北京上百万人上街是不能相比的。”

另外，从诉求上来讲，“六四”的诉求从一开始就很清楚，就是要国家向人权、向民主、向反腐败这个方向迈进。76年的4·5事件基本上是表达对毛泽东以及党内极左派所谓的“四人帮”的不满，以及悼念敬爱的周总理的框架下展开的。

89年的学运从规模上讲遍及全国各地，光北京就有上百万人上街。天安门广场最多的时候是数万人。

宋永毅教授说：“从这个规模上讲，从它的诉求，和从它在组织上的井然有序来讲，可以说是规模最大的学生运动，而且是民主运动。这个历史史实恐怕是没有什么可以争议的。”

89学运时期学生提出的七条要求中最重要的两条是：国家领导人及其家属年薪及一切形式的收入向人民公开，反对贪官污吏；允许民间办报，解除报禁，实行言论自由。

这两条要求在25年后的今天仍未兑现。现今中国官商勾结，贪腐横行。尽管习近平决心下大力气反腐，但许志永等一些公开呼吁官员财产公示的活动人士却锒铛入狱，当局对媒体和互联网的管控日益加剧，对舆论控制有增无减。

香港知名媒体人梁文道在他的一篇名为《犬儒時代的信任》的博文中写道： “这个社会将不只是个信任匮乏的社会，它还必将滋生出一种犬儒的冷漠。对于一种明明背离了现实的描述和形容，他们会说：‘这真是没办法’；他们会说：‘反正事情就是这样了’。默然地承受，机敏地度量，以一已的智巧去处理世事之无奈。”

宋永毅教授说：“正是因为‘六四’（学运）被镇压，（中国）进入了黑暗时期，进入了一个犬儒时代，整个腐败堕落就这么来了。与今天的腐败相比，‘六四’时代可以说是一个廉政时代了。”

不过，袁伟时教授认为，现在的贪腐程度之所以大于“六四”时期是因为中国的市场经济在25年前还没有充分发展，尽管中国政府仍然严控舆论和互联网，但不得不承认老百姓的言论空间还是比10年前扩大了许多。

也许有人会说，“六四”学运的影响有限，因为中国目前还没有变成民主。

对此，宋永毅教授表示，对于研究历史的学者来说，不一定是“成者为王，败者为寇”。有的时候，它的失败比成功的意义更大。应该说，当时历史的成功与“六四”是擦肩而过。

他说：“虽然（‘六四’学运）在中国失败了，但在整个社会主义阵营是胜利了。‘苏东波’（与‘六四’）是同时举行的，六四失败了，但他们胜利了。而最近发生的一系列事情，还是和‘六四’有关，最近抓人呀等等，可见这个斗争还会延续下去。”
历史教训

由此可见，“六四”已经成为中共最大的一个历史包袱，重新评价“六四”是他们最大的忌讳。

25年过去后，经历过这段历史的人或集体失忆失声，或被失忆失声。没有经历这段历史的人也无从寻找六四的历史痕迹。

反思六四学运，它的最大的历史教训是什么呢？

广州中山大学历史教授袁伟时认为，最大的历史教训就是朝野双方面对矛盾的时候都要有很宽容的态度，认真寻求双赢的局面。在他看来，中国的执政党仍然没有跳出“革命”与“反革命”，“夺权”与“反夺权”的思维模式，而疏于具体的制度建设。他说，“六四”时期的执政当局很不冷静，采取了很不妥当的措施。但另一方面，民间也是不知道什么是适可而止。

袁伟时教授说，在“六四”时期，学生们应该如何与一个无产阶级专政体系打交道是一个很大的问题。但当时的学生和支持学运的知识阶层在这方面都没有一个足够的研究和智慧。

“相反，作为执政党来说，面对公民提出的强烈要求，他们认为是不能接受，对他们来说是一种威胁。他们也无法正确处理这样一个局面。因此，这两个方面碰在一起，就演变成一个特大悲剧。”

袁伟时曾在一篇文章中写道：“文明与野蛮永无止息的争斗，是人类历史的基本内容。每一个历史阶段，都有几个国家率先树立了文明的标杆。跟进还是抗拒？令人眼花缭乱的史剧围绕着这个轴心展开。”

“六四”，也许就是这一史剧的真实写照。

Hey @marcusjv-po - so this isn't really a stacker thing so much as a troposphere, and more importantly, an underlying cloudformation thing.  Lets take a simple example - an s3 bucket.  You can, if you want, control the bucket name by providing `BucketName` (almost all resources with "names" have something similar): https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-s3-bucket.html#cfn-s3-bucket-name

However, Cloudformation has a *big* gotcha with human named resources (you'll notice a link to this page from every resource with a Name property, btw):

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-name.html

Basically, if any update requires a replacement (and many do for good reason) you can't update that resource because Cloudformation can't create a new name for you, and the way cloudformation replaces something is:

1. create new resource with new name
2. delete old resource

Since names are unique, it can't create #1 if you provide a static name.

That said, for the most part, I've found this isn't a bad thing.  Many resources actually have a tag called `Name`, and if you set that it's almost always shown in the console.  In the end, Cloudformation, and stacker by extension, tends to take the "treat your resources like cattle not pets" approach, which is surprisingly pretty easy to get used to in the long run.

Hope that helps!  let me know if you have any other questions!

(PS: BTW, because of the trickiness with names, I tend to reject open source blueprints that allow setting them in stacker_blueprints - it's too easy to shoot yourself in the foot, and not realize it till some point in the future, so I avoid them in the open source provided examples).

There is a difference between bucketName and the name of the resource.

This works:

    bucket = s3.Bucket('NoHyphens', BucketName='with-hyphens')

This doesn't:

    bucket = s3.Bucket('Invalid-Hyphen', BucketName='valid-hyphen').

Are we sure the length calculations haven't always been like this?

To me the lengths should be the length of symbols, which have been generated. In the sequences NDArray we include the <s> symbol, however it is not a symbol that gets generated by the model and it should therefore not count towards the length. That said, it would maybe be more consistent to return the sequences NDArray without <s>. With this in mind the length in the example given below is correct though if we discount for `0` and `2`.

For beam search I think it is actually the correct thing to use the old lengths array for normalizing for the fact that the lengths array is initialized to `1` instead of `0`. After generating the first real symbol we should normalize the score by `length_penalty(1)` as we have only produced a single score thus far.

So I think if we use the old lengths array we are doing the correct thing. That said, it is currently inconsistent and confusing what we do, so that there is potential for some refactoring/cleanup (that however should not change the way we score).

For development environment you often will spin up the templates multiple times creating various log groups that will clutter your overview with (in time) non relevant log groups. Creating the Log Group from the CloudFormation template will remove the logs automatically.

For production environments it's always good to know what the retention is and explicitly setting it makes it real clear, if you want to archive them glacier would be a cheaper solution when dealing with large amounts of logs.

That being said if you decide to implement this you probably always want to deploy a LogGroup because if the log group has been created by an invocation you cannot create it in a update on a later time. So to keep the existing behavior a `DeletionPolicy: retain` (Not sure if this is supported) can be set and then you need to be able to control it something like:

```Function:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      LogRetention: 7
      LogDeletionPolicy: retain
```

Okay so here's how it works right now:

1. Create instance of Django TestCase and add it to `context.test`
2. Run `behave_run_hook` which will run your `before_scenario` function
3. Setup the test case with `test._pre_setup()` and etc.

Your `before_scenario` function needs to run before `_pre_setup()` because `before_scenario` is where you initialize fixtures, and `_pre_setup()` is where Django loads them to the test database.

Unfortunately, `_pre_setup()` is also where Django initializes its test client. So anything you assign to `test.client` will get overwritten.

Sorry guys, I don't think there's a way to fix this without breaking fixtures. That said, this will only happen if you use specifically use `test.client`. You could still use a different name. Maybe `test.api` or `test.api_client`.

At the moment you'd need to create a custom `com.mycompany.Divider` transformation. The [SkLearn2PMML-Plugin](https://github.com/jpmml/sklearn2pmml-plugin) project provides "rails" for that - shouldn't take more than an hour or two.

In the long run, there should be some sort of `sklearn2pmml.preprocessing.ExpressionTransformer` transformation for doing simple math work. You would write the expression as a string, which would be `eval()`-ed on the Python side, and be parsed/translated on the PMML/Java side:
```Python
expr = ExpressionTransformer("X[0] / X[1]")
Xt = expr.fit_transform(X)
```

By "valid" I mean "able to be represented in HTML". I'm saying it's not possible to represent U+0001 in HTML. 

It can't be represented by numeric character references (see https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/syntax.html#character-references):

> The numeric character reference forms described above are allowed to reference any Unicode code point other than U+0000, U+000D, permanently undefined Unicode characters (noncharacters), surrogates (U+D800–U+DFFF), and control characters other than space characters.

Nor can they be represented by encoding them (see https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/syntax.html#preprocessing-the-input-stream):

> Any occurrences of any characters in the ranges U+0001 to U+0008, …snip… are parse errors. These are all control characters or permanently undefined Unicode characters (noncharacters).

* Nuking all files in the `bug_metrics/migrations` directory

* Then fake out the migration, as it is already in a known state:

txaio is a compatibility library so we can write "the same" code across asyncio and Twisted. The logging system in here is built as a mostly-thin wrapper over Twisted's new logging system, giving us semi-structured logging -- that is, you can completely ignore the format string and just log the `kwargs` you get (which will be objects).

This also has speed benefits: because the string interpolation is delayed, un-logged levels (e.g. .trace or .debug) never get interpolated into strings.

If you really do want to do your own string-interpolation stuff, you can follow a pattern like this: ``log.info("{msg}", msg="some %s" % ("thing",))``

That's "interesting" output, but also that's not how the logging should be used -- you should use the `{name}` syntax, like so:

```python
logging.info('Some dict output: {a}', a=1)
```
We mention the support Python versions in the README, and we have Tox envs / Travis for all supported variants, but the `setup.py` lacks specifiers.

I think maybe what you want to do is this:

 - not call `start_logging` at all
 - set up your own handlers (or observers for Twisted)
 - call txaio's `set_global_log_level()` method
 - start the global logging thing (`beginLoggingTo(observers)` in Twisted)

If that does work for your use-case, the remaining part will be to ensure that txaio functions properly (and the logs are emitted correctly) if you call ONLY `set_global_log_level`. That's the piece that turns "not the right level" calls into no-ops, which is fairly critical for Autobahn (because we want "trace" and "debug" statements to be no or extremely-little overhead).

Overall, I'm not clear how we want to move forward or reach a decision.  While I think any route forward is better than the status quo, I'm not particularly inclined to make a PR for a route I wouldn't choose.

1.  Matt G and Michael's first opinion is to advance the Spacy version.
2.  Matt P's opinion is that we should pin Spacy to 2.0.5.
3.  Mark N's opinion is that we should remove the test case so people can use any version of Spacy 2.

Typically I don't strive for consensus and instead evaluate CR's on whether they make the codebase worse or not, rather than whether they are the best way forward.  If we merge this, but someone wants to make another PR for another direction, great.  Alternatively if someone else wants to put forth a PR with another solution, that's fine too.

        echo "command must be run as: $0 --really"
        exit 1
      fi
      # procedure requires the store-admin
      ADMIN_RUNNING=$(docker inspect --format="{{ .State.Running }}" deis-store-admin)
      if [ $? -eq 1 ] || [ "$ADMIN_RUNNING" == "false" ]; then
        echo "deis-store-admin container is required for graceful shutdown"
        exit 2
      fi
      set -e -x -o pipefail
      # determine osd id
      CURRENT_STATUS=$(/usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health | awk '{print $1}')
      OSD_HOSTS=($(/usr/bin/etcdctl ls /deis/store/hosts/| awk -F'/' '{print $5}'))
      for HOST in "${OSD_HOSTS[@]}"
      do
        PUBLIC_IP=$(fleetctl list-machines -fields="machine,ip" -full -no-legend| grep 61ea45444f894e989de49638ef98ec6f | awk '{print $2}')
        if [ "$HOST" = "$PUBLIC_IP" ] ; then
          OSD_ID=$(/usr/bin/etcdctl get /deis/store/osds/$PUBLIC_IP)
          break
        fi
      done
      # if we own an osd and its healthy, try to gracefully remove it
      if [ ! -z "$OSD_ID" ] && [[ "$CURRENT_STATUS" == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]] && [ ${#OSD_HOSTS[@]} -gt "3" ]; then
        /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd out $OSD_ID
        sleep 30
        TIMEWAITED=0
        until [[ $(/usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph health) == *"HEALTH_OK"* ]]
        do
          if [ $TIMEWAITED -gt "1200" ]
          then
            echo "ceph graceful removal timeout exceeded"
            break
          fi
          echo "waiting" && sleep 5
          TIMEWAITED=$((TIMEWAITED+5))
        done
        /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-daemon
        /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd crush remove osd.$OSD_ID
        /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph auth del osd.$OSD_ID
        /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph osd rm $OSD_ID
        /usr/bin/etcdctl rm /deis/store/osds/$PUBLIC_IP
        etcdctl rm /deis/store/hosts/$PUBLIC_IP && sleep 10
        # remove ceph mon
        /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-monitor || true
        /usr/bin/docker exec deis-store-admin ceph mon remove coreos-ue59lyhnxjww7qfi # fixme
        /usr/bin/docker stop deis-store-metadata || true
      fi
      NODE=$(curl -L http://127.0.0.1:7001/v2/admin/machines/61ea45444f894e989de49638ef98ec6f)

        /usr/bin/curl -L -XDELETE http://127.0.0.1:7001/v2/admin/machines/61ea45444f894e989de49638ef98ec6f
      fi

Trying to put some closure on this.  Collectively, I think we have established and agree that etcd is behaving just as intended and as its own documentation describes.  etcd documentation further recommends (and I believe this recommendation ought to be taken seriously) that etcd peers run on a small, fixed number of nodes that use static configuration instead of the etcd discovery service.  A corollary to this is that these few nodes should not be managed in an auto-scaling group.

While Deis' existing provisioning processes do not account for this, etcd and Deis are both sufficiently documented such that anyone wanting to run such a configuration should be able to provision infrastructure to that end, albeit not without some effort.

We're currently investigating options for a more production-grade provisioning process (on AWS only to start), but I do believe the OP's proposal that "New nodes should be members, not proxies, on the etcd cluster," has been adequately dispelled as something we should not do, favoring instead to _not_ autoscale etcd peers.  Based on that I'm going to close this.

> I was reading here about callbacks: http://toolkit.globus.org/toolkit/docs/3.2/developer/globus-async-example2.html
> 
> Specifically, is there functionality in the Python SDK (or other) to give the request a callback URL / webhook so that when a transfer is finished, the local server gets a ping that the image is there?

That documentation is for the underlying gridftp software that's running on the endpoints themselves. It would only be useful if you were looking to extend gridftp itself.

The Transfer API doesn't have any sort of async notification mechanism (e.g., webhooks) so your best bet may be polling on the task to check its status. The has a `task_wait` helper that may be useful: https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/clients/transfer/#globus_sdk.TransferClient.task_wait.

Also, to clear up the confusion about using the `href` in the response from the API, it is relative to the Transer API's base URL, which is `https://transfer.api.globus.org/v0.10/` so if you used https://transfer.api.globus.org/v0.10/task/7878dcbe-3f18-11e8-ba20-0ac6873fc732 you would get task data as a response.

I do update conda frequently and this time it did not work. I had to reinstall anaconda

> the reason I included Python 3.5 was that conda create -n test ipython=4.2 pytest defaulted to Python 2.7 (even though my conda is itself in Python 3.5).

Not sure why that be, but I'm going to set that aside for the moment because that's a separate issue from the solver itself (it's an `add_defaults_to_specs` issue, most likely).

> I converted the environment to Python 2.7 with conda install python=2.7. This chose IPython 1.0 (!) from my channel.

Yes. That's because it's preferring _any_ version of iPython in your channel over _any_ version in the lower-priority `defaults` channel. Forcing `ipython=4.2` made it drop down to `defaults` to pick a compatible one.

This would be a good argument for changing the channel priority behavior to _ignore_ packages from a lower-priority channel. But this must also coincide with the ability to explicitly specify the channel you want to pull a package from. We're working on that.

Moral of this story, for now: don't keep packages in your personal channel that you don't intend to use.

> Updating IPython didn't update its dependencies. There's a reason the update_dependencies option was set to default to true.

Well, we might disagree on what `update_dependencies` should do. As we have it now, it's primary function is to _prevent_ dependencies from being updated when set to `False`, for users who don't want to change any other packages in their environment. 

When it is `True`, we are *not* giving Conda carte blanche to update everything. Instead, `conda` updates dependences *on an as-needed basis*. When a dependency *does* need to be updated, it is updated as much as possible; but if the current dependency is sufficient to handle the requested package, it is not touched. And according to `conda info ipython=4.2.0=py27_1`, all of the dependencies currently in your environment at this point were satisfactory.

We went back and forth on this when the new solver was being demonstrated. People were quite concerned that the more aggressive dependency resolution was effectively going to force far too many updates.

So conda is behaving as expected here. I'd argue this is a bug in `ipython`'s conda recipe.

> Both conda install six and conda update six didn't update six. I would expect at least one, if not both to do it.

That's because it's the latest version of `six` in your channel. Again, it behaved as expected here.

It looks like you'd be a good candidate for `channel_priority: False`. This way, you'll get the latest versions of the packages, whether or not they come from your channel. With that setting, the sort order is version, then channel, then build (because build numbers are not comparable across channels).

The other thing one can do to always pull `conda` from the `defaults` channel, while otherwise using `conda-forge` is to pin it only for the base env:
```
conda activate base
conda config --env --add pinned_packages defaults::conda
```
The under-advertised `--env` option enables you to have environment-dependent configurations of your channel priorities.
You can check what effect this command had by executing:

```
conda config --show-sources
```

@kraigher Looking at it from the user's perspective, I would rather log that VUnit saw a duplicate file attempted to be added and then not add it rather than raise an exception.  If an exception is raised, then the user still has to handle the exception anyway (which was my workaround to this issue in the first place). Thanks for contributing to Curator!  I have a concern. Have you tried to rollover an index named `crazy_test-%Y.%m.%d`?  

Elasticsearch does not allow you to rollover an index not ending with a dash and a number, e.g. `-1`, even with named indices.  The test you provided may work, but the problem would arise when you tried to rollover the resulting index named `crazy_test-%Y.%m.%d`.

See [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.5/indices-rollover-index.html#_using_date_math_with_the_rollover_api):

> It can be useful to use date math to name the rollover index according to the date that the index rolled over, e.g. logstash-2016.02.03. The rollover API supports date math, but requires the index name to end with a dash followed by a number, e.g. logstash-2016.02.03-1 which is incremented every time the index is rolled over.

The emphasis should be on the phrase, **but requires the index name to end with a dash followed by a number.**  It may work if your date string uses hyphens/dashes, but the reality is that if you _do_ use that date format, then testing for this condition quickly gets messy and ugly.

This patch can still be merged, but a check to see if the index name ends with a dash followed by a number would have to be added, along with unit tests (positive and negative), and hopefully integration tests also verifying the same behavior.

Did you check the resulting file that was uploaded? In my case it succeeded and created a file in azure, but it was empty.

The repro case we have is basically piping ~400GB of tarball into the following script. I don't think any of what it's doing should matter, but I could be wrong.
```
#!/usr/local/bin/zsh -e

# This is a shell script that is conceptually what we want:
# STDIN -> pigz -> gpg -> tee shasum -> split 100G -> tee (s3, azure) -> /dev/null
# We need to split it into 100G chunks because azure can't accept blobs bigger than 200G.
# Important to print the output of split into /dev/null, because we handle what we actually want
# to do withb the split output in the SPLITSCRIPT - and otherwise, we back up the pipeline because nothing
# is consuming the stdout of the pipeline.
# We want to connect the stderror's to the ruby logger, so we will define a bunch of pipes for errors to
# end up in, each of which has a thread printing to the logger.

set -e # and exit when it does
setopt pipefail
renice -n 10 -p $$ >&2 # It would be bad if replicas fell behind

echo "About to kick off pipeline" >&2

export SHELL="/usr/local/bin/zsh" # `split` uses this var to decide what shell to spawn

# This is kind of a sad problem with tee(1)
# It reads from stdin, terminates once it has written all of stdin to its arguments.
# if you do something like cmd-1 | tee <(cmd-2) | cmd-3, the shell waits for cmd-1,
# tee, and cmd-3 to finish before running the next line of the script. Unfortunately,
# tee has no way to wait for cmd-2 to finish, and bash doesn't care. This means that
# it will still be running when the script finishes.
# In practical terms, this means that doing ... | tee <(upload-to-s3) <(upload-to-azure) won't
# actually wait for the upload to finish, and the last part of the upload on s3/azure will actually
# never appear in the bucket, because the upload is not finalised.
# zsh on the other hand is polite enough to wait for the redirection to finish, so we'll go with that.

cat | \
    pigz -5 2>"$GZIP_ERR" | \
    gpg --batch --encrypt --sign --compress-level 0 --recipient "$ENCRYPT_KEY" --local-user "$SIGNING_KEY" --trust-model always 2>"$GPG_ERR" | \
    # Make a shasum, but pipe it into sed to replace "abcdef0123 -" with "abcdef0123 snapshot_file.tar.gz.gpg" so that it is a valid SHA256SUMS file
    { tee >(sha256sum - 2>"$SHASUM_ERR" | sed "s/-$/${BARE_FILE_NAME}/" >"$SHASUM_OUT" 2>"$SHASUM_ERR"); } 2>"$TEE_ERR" | \
    split --bytes 107374182400 --numeric-suffixes --filter='
        setopt pipefail;
        set -e;

        # Do this logic inside the split filter, because we cannot expoort an array as an env var
        if [[ -n "$OBJECT_SHOULD_EXPIRE_DATE" ]]; then
            S3_EXPIRY_FLAGS=("--expires" "$OBJECT_SHOULD_EXPIRE_DATE")
        else
            S3_EXPIRY_FLAGS=()
        fi

        { tee \
            >(
                aws s3 cp - "$S3_OBJECT_URI.$FILE" --expected-size 268435456000 --storage-class STANDARD_IA "${S3_EXPIRY_FLAGS[@]}" 2>"$S3_ERR" 1>&2
            ) \
            >(
                python2.7 -m azure.cli storage blob upload -f /dev/fd/0 -n "$AZURE_OBJECT_NAME.$FILE" \
                    --container-name "$AZURE_CONTAINER_NAME" --account-key "$AZURE_STORAGE_ACCESS_KEY" \
                    --account-name "$AZURE_STORAGE_ACCOUNT" --max-connections 1 2>"$AZ_ERR" 1>&2
            ) \
            >(
                sha256sum - 2>"$SHASUM_ERR" | sed "s/-$/${BARE_FILE_NAME}.${FILE}/" >"$SHASUM_OUT" 2>"$SHASUM_ERR"
            );
        } 2>"$TEE_ERR" >/dev/null
        exit 0;
    ' - 'part_' >/dev/null

```

Yes, I've made the PR. When one tries getting the JSON data, `Content-Type` is first check and `HTTPNotAcceptable` error is raised if it is not `application/json`. One could also switch off the checking or write a handler himself/herself. In comparison with Flask, [getting json requests is more sophisticated](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/api/#flask.Request.get_json), and I don't know whether this kind of way you would prefer. Also, [Ruby On Rails](http://rubyonrails.org/) would automatically detect the type of the request and allows one to [render separated results over the same controller](http://guides.rubyonrails.org/action_controller_overview.html#rendering-xml-and-json-data) corresponding to the type. Maybe you could add this to aiohttp so that one can save a lot of extra code.

Well, if your model isn't differentiable w.r.t. its inputs, it's hard to apply a gradient-based adversarial attack. The problem with the LocalSearchAttack is: it has built-in notion of pixels and is therefore image-specific. I do think it should be possible to generalize the attack to arbitrary inputs, but I haven't thought about it in detail. Maybe you want to give it a try? Alternatively, it might be possible to change the shape of your data to something that looks like an image, e.g. an image with width 1, height N and 1 color channel. Again, no guarantees, but I think that should be easy and might work. Finally, you can use a decision-based attack: it requires even less than the `LocalSearchattack`. To get started and make sure that your code works, I recommend something basic like the `AdditiveUniformNoiseAttack`.  Once everything works, you can use our recently published `BoundaryAttack`. https://foolbox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modules/attacks/decision.html

What's the use case for the builtins tuple/list/dict?

Surely the idea with the numpy/scipy primitives and with other builtins like type and isinstance is that you can directly substitute the autograd wrapped versions into pre-existing user code and it 'just works'.

With list and dict at least this won't be the case, since the boxes returned by autograd.builtins.list and autograd.builtins.dict are immutable. As a trivial example the following function would break if you used autograd.builtins.list in place of list:
```python
def f(x):
    l = list((x, x, x))
    l[0] = 0
    return sum(l)
```

> Ok, I see what you're saying. Your point is that we can't tell users to always use the Autograd version of everything, because the Autograd tuple/list/dict is worse than the Python tuple/list/dict in many respects.

Yeah, I think it's a fairly minor point but one worth making.

> I had assumed that we would tell users to use tuple/list/dict from Autograd.builtins only as needed.

I'm also not clear on when these functions would be needed.

The strategy I'm pursuing now is simply to try to call `hess()` less by keeping track of which subsystems have converged, i.e., let `converged` be a boolean array of shape `(100, 100)` and let `points` be an input array of shape `(100, 100, 10)`. Then you can do something like

```python
hessian[np.nonzero(~converged) + np.index_exp[:, :]] = hess(*points[np.nonzero(~converged) + np.index_exp[:]])
```

and just update `converged` on every iteration according to some criteria. I haven't tested it yet but I expect I'll see a nice performance increase since the minimizer right now runs in lockstep -- i.e., if even one of the `100 * 100` subsystems hasn't converged, it recomputes the Hessian of all subsystems. It made prototyping easy but it definitely hurts performance.

I agree that jvp/vjp is confusing, although if we change those it should probably be to something more distinct than jo/jao.  Here are a couple of possibilities:

fwd/rev (not very classy or descriptive, but at least memorable)
drd/adj (directional derivative and adjoint)

By the way, I'm still not clear on the motivation for JTPs.  Is it just for things like jacobian?  In principle, if every jvp supported broadcasting along its first dimension, would we need jtps?

Unfortunately, Python loops are slow, and we'd have to work to generalize that solution to arbitrary dimensions (remember, `x` could have any shape and the product could be along any axis).

Doing a bidirectional `np.cumprod` is probably the most asymptotically time- and memory-efficient way, and it's easy to pass in the `axis` argument there for generality (though I see we don't have its gradient implemented yet).

What you describe to be the problem seems to be spot on:  When one element is zero, then the method used to reconstruct partial derivative (i.e., smth like `np.prod(a) / a[i]`) results in 0 / 0, i.e. undefined.  The only way to correctly reconstruct the partial derivative in such case is to have access to all the elements in `a`.

I've had a look at the exponential backoff algorithm in listen_forever.

It's not as bad as it looks. The retry time will be 5000 seconds only if the first ever sync fails. If it keeps failing, it goes to retry every 3600 seconds (1 hour).

If it succeeds, the timeout resets back to 5 seconds. It will double each time it fails, up to the maximum of 3600, and resets back to 5 as soon as it succeeds again.

So all in all, it's not too bad, but it's not right either. I agree that it is most likely that 5 was meant instead of 5000 in https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/blob/aa17a6a902b0928b3eee2ebd1bd9c8c7ef95c27b/matrix_client/client.py#L424

Between 2.0 and 2.1 we were still working the kinks out of our symbol publishing story.   There were bugs in either the way symbols were generated and/or how we automatically published them.    Some of these builds we fixed things by hand, but there are now 9 different 2.0.X releases and we DON'T like to change things in servicing releases.   

The upshot is that because it is so easy to upgrade and we KNOW that 2.1 works properly, the value of hand fixing every 2.0.X release is dubious (and decreases as time goes on).  

All of our releases are here https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/release-notes/download-archive.md.  Ideally you install the latest 2.1 release if you want to debug through the framework.  (they are side by side (although it does roll forward by default).   

You could make a case that 2.0.7, which is the latest (and maybe last 2.0.X release), should work (and I agree with that since it currently is our latest 'officially released' version) if that does not have proper symbols, we should fix it by hand). 

But if you want to be sure it works, just use 2.1.

I finally decided to try Let’s Encrypt on my personal Ubuntu 12.04 LTS server and followed the [How it works](https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/) documentation as a quick guide to “Getting Started”.

After cloning the git repository, I ran the `letsencrypt-auto –help` command and was very much taken aback by the way it installed `augeas-lenses` and `libaugeas0` packages with a number of python libraries and created a Python 2.7 virtual environment in `~/.local/share/letsencrypt`.

While I’m aware that this is beta software, this breaks the [principle of least surprise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment) and I agree with others that running any command with the `--help` option should only print a help message without requiring superuser privileges to do so.

I’d much prefer the application to print a message stating that not all the necessary components are installed and to either:

1. print a message asking me to manually install those packages
2. include a `--auto-update` or `--self-update` option to automatically install the necessary software and update itself.

At the very least, it should prompt the user to *ask for permission*.

I later discovered the [User Guide](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#plugins) which warns that running `./letsencrypt-auto` without any options will install dependencies and create the virtual environment and from reading [Discourse threads](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/i-ran-letsencrypt-auto-help-and-it-used-sudo-to-install-packages/7639), I’ve learned that

> `letsencrypt-auto` itself does not have a `--help` switch. It's just a wrapper around the `letsencrypt` client. Your --help switch would have been passed to that command, but would not have bypassed all the installation parts of the wrapper script.

I think this could be made clearer in the [documentation](http://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#letsencrypt-auto).

@jbrockmendel Not quite. `fold` is an attribute for distinguishing between two otherwise identical dates, e.g. 1:30 AM EDT vs 1:30 AM EST during an EDT->EST transition. So:

`'2011-11-06T01:30 EDT'`
`'2011-11-06T01:30 EST'`

These two things are unambiguous representations (assuming you have the context to know that EST and EDT are both representations of the Eastern time zone) of the same naive datetime. With Python's time zone model, the tz offset is a function of the naive `datetime` portion, so for dates like the above differing only in their offset, there was no way to represent the difference with the same `tzinfo` object. In Python 3.6, they introduced `fold`, so you can represent those two, respectively, as:

`datetime(2011, 11, 6, 1, 30, fold=0, tzinfo=Eastern)`
`datetime(2011, 11, 6, 1, 30, fold=1, tzinfo=Eastern)`

In the old behavior, it used to be that we'd see `EST` or `EDT`, find that those were aliases for the local time zone name, and assign `tzlocal()`, but of course we didn't assign the `fold` component, because it didn't exist until recently. This patch just checks to see if the `tzname` doesn't match the string token but a datetime with `fold=1` set *would* match the string token, that we set `fold=1`.

Hi d-fence,
   The way to answer this question is to build a lookup table of all the known files and their allocation blocks and then search the lookup table for a file which might contain this block. You can either build the map in memory, or a better option is to build it into a database of some sort (e.g. sqlite) because that would take some time to pre-calculate. Then you lookup the block and see if it appears inside any file. If not it may be in unallocated area.

If you decide to do it in memory, a nice module to do this is intervaltree which you can just pip install: 
https://pypi.python.org/pypi/intervaltree/2.0.4

The second part of the problem is to iterate over all files and collect their runs. You can check out the code in the examples/istat.py example for this:
```
f = fs.open_meta(inode = inode)
t = IntervalTree()

for attr in f:
    for run in attr:
        t[run.addr:run.addr + run.len] = inode
```

Or something similar using sqlite.

If you do end up writing some code we would appreciate a contribution to the examples directory. 

Thanks,

Hi @bobdillon48, please see the [User guide chapter](https://apple.github.io/turicreate/docs/userguide/object_detection/#data-acquisition) on Object Detection, which gives examples of the JSON format used for describing the bounding boxes. The bounding box description for a single row (where each image's metadata is a row of your CSV), would look something like this:

```json
[{"coordinates": {"height": 104, "width": 110, "x": 115, "y": 216}, "label": "ball"}]
```

Note that there could be more than one object annotation per image, which is why this is a JSON array. I think a single object is also valid.

To construct a CSV file that would describe each image this way, you'd want two columns, one for the path to the image (relative paths from within a directory where you will execute the script tends to work best), and one for the annotations. The resulting CSV file would look something like:


```csv
path,annotations
images/image1.jpg,[{"coordinates": {"height": 104, "width": 110, "x": 115, "y": 216}, "label": "ball"}]
images/image2.jpg,[{"coordinates": {"height": 208, "width": 40, "x": 48, "y": 29}, "label": "tree"}]
```

Then, you can read the resulting file into SFrame format with `turicreate.SFrame.read_csv` and join it to your images with `join` (assuming the path column name is the same, and contains the same image paths, in both SFrames).

I'm also going to leave this issue open and tag as `docs`, so we can use it to track updating the documentation to make it clearer. Hey, yeah in our case the app is still using the old Django profile system rather than a custom user model, but that's not entirely relevant to the issue I'm reporting.

DRF allows you to expose any attribute you feel fit as the 'id' in a serializer. By default it uses the 'pk' attribute of the model, but this can be changed like (see below). All I would like is for this functionality to be exposed by this library, currently it's hard-coded to the `pk` of the model. In merge request #165 I've taken a stab at it by simply grabbing the `pk` field from the serializers output.

```python
class Thing(ModelSerializer):
    id = serializers.CharField(source='some_arbitrary_attribute')

    class Meta:
        fields = ('id',)
        model = Thing
```

My particular use case is a bit convoluted, but the core of it is that this library naively assumes that you only want to expose the `pk` of a model as is, when DRF allows you to expose anything.

It is not a scenario that opt solver handles. Quantifiers will not work either. Perhaps I print a warning in this case, but there is no simple way to deal with quantified hard constraints when they influence the objective values.

The straightforward way to enumerate all models is to ask the solver multiple times, in each iteration adding a new constraint that negates the last found model. I.e., assert a new constraint of the form `(not (= a 1) (= b 0) ...)` and then run `check` again, until the problem becomes unsatisfiable.

Python has two concepts of packages: file-as-a-package (FAAP) and directory-as-a-package (DAAP). FAAP is used for one-file throw-away things, DAAP is preferred for packages that contain many internal files (like z3 bindings).

You know how FAAP works: put everything you need in `z3.py` and use `import z3` in your scripts.

For DAAP, place all python code is a directory `z3`, and create a file `__init__.py` in that directory. Then, `import z3` will import `__init__.py` from `z3` directory. 

When  converting from FAAP to DAAP and want to preserve existing behavior, you have two choices:

1. move the content of `z3.py` into `z3/__init__.py`
2. copy z3.py into directory `z3`. In `__init__.py` add `from z3 import *`. In this case, the statement `import z3` in your scripts imports `__init__.py` and the statement `from z3...` in `__init__.py` imports `z3.py` at the top level.

Whether you want to convert the bindings library into DAAP is your choice. It would be cleaner, but might be disruptive to current users. I've done it in the past for personal use, but have never tested it heavily.

Hope this helps,
arie

```python
X -= (0.5, 2.0 / n_batch / max_length)
y -= 1.0
```

Epoch 190 validation cost = 0.00439414521679
Epoch 191 validation cost = 0.00428196648136

Epoch 197 validation cost = 0.00445842323825

Epoch 199 validation cost = 0.00411068694666
```
Much better and still not overfitting, but not solved according to the 0.04 absolute error metric.

The docstring change should be in a separate commit this time, i.e., not squashed into the first one. Let me know when you're done so we can merge! (github doesn't send out a notification for additional commits to a PR, only for additional comments.)

> I wonder why a symmetrical architecture would be better. Does it lead to a reduction in reconstruction error or maybe some other theoretical aspects?

Well, it probably doesn't have to be exactly symmetrical, but in your architecture, the decoder is a lot more expressive than the encoder. You're basically training an elaborate decoder to make sense of codes hastily thrown together. The analogy may be a bit far-fetched, but in regular compression algorithms, encoding takes a lot more cycles than decoding -- so if anything, I'd expect the decoder to have fewer layers, not the encoder.

> Maybe we should add a test or two to make sure we support scalar parameters in general? Seems like an easy thing to break inadvertently :)

The easiest (albeit a bit indirect) way would be to add a layer to Lasagne that uses scalar parameters, and have tests for that (as well as include that layer in tests of the various helper functions). We could also add direct tests for `lasagne.utils.floatX` et al., but I'm not sure how helpful that is.

In any case, I'll merge this one already.

Maybe somebody else has a good idea... we have `l1` and `l2`, both of which take a tensor expression and return a scalar expression, and we want to provide an easy way to apply that to a network output, the regularizable parameters of one or more layers, or the regularizable parameters of a full network.

What about:
```python
def apply_penalty(things, penalty, **kwargs):

```

The use cases would be:
```python

loss = apply_penalty(layer.get_params(regularizable=True), l2)
loss = apply_penalty((layer.get_params(regularizable=True) for layer in layers), l2)
loss = apply_penalty(get_all_params(layer, regularizable=True), l2)
```

Another alternative is to move the handling of default values into the constructor body. Yes. The `BatchNormLayer` should be implemented similarly to the `BatchNormDNNLayer`, and then we could ditch the latter. We can also think about providing a way to use `std` instead of `inv_std` so we can leave all updates to cuDNN if the arguments allow.

> Is there anybody interested in this implementation in Lasagne?

The existing `BatchNormLayer` can already do layer normalization by just changing the `axis` specification, but then you'd always need to call `get_output()` with `batch_norm_use_averages=False, batch_norm_compute_averages=False`. So we could either add a `LayerNormLayer` (uh...) or make the running averages of `BatchNormLayer` completely optional and just add a `layernorm()` convenience function that instantiates a `BatchNormLayer`. Hmm, or maybe add a `NormLayer` for layer normalization and derive `BatchNormLayer` from it, just adding the running average statistics on top and changing the default `axis`. I think that might be the cleanest option. Sorry for thinking aloud.

We had a parallel discussion about the location of `id` and `last_modified` (which we named *metadata*). There are multiple reasons to put them in a different location than the data itself (one of them being that the clients currently need to mutate the returned objects to pass them to their consumers) and we were thinking about putting them in a `metadata` namespace.

I was concerned about the complexity it adds to the API, and that's the reason why I like your proposal: it separates the metadata from the data itself, without adding yet another namespace.

I would propose that we do that by iterations:

1. Move the `schema` attribute to the root of the collection object. Leave the schema attribute on records as it is for now;
2. Move `id` and `last_modified` to the root as well;
3. Move the `schema` attribute of records to the root.

This change would impact both Kinto.js, and Syncto. If we have to break the API, better do it sooner than later: nobody is currently using this protocol for something that shipped. If we want/have to wait, we'll need to think handle backward compatibility, which isn't a concern we have right now.

Also, there is something I don't get: why are you saying this is that complex? What do you identify as the potential implementation problems?

The name could be something like `inverse_tranform` rather than `inverse` to help make it clearer that this runs the inverse *transformation* (which should only produce the original data if the coefficients were unmodified).

@WardLT that is a good point, with one correction: we need more than the composition to determine the maximum bag lengths, we need the structure. We basically need to 

(1) calculate the number of each kind of bond in each _structure_ (the "bag length")
(2) for each bond, select the largest bag length from among all fitted structures

So I suppose that could be a todo for BoB: determine the bag lengths without doing the CM computation.

However I also think just due to the nature of BoB, something in the spirit of 'fit_featurize' might be nice, because (a) the *number of features is variable* with the set of structures to be featurized but (b)   'fitting' on one set of structures and 'featurizing' on another set is probably going to screw up any BoB featurizer implementation. You'll probably always want to fit and featurize on the _same dataframe_ in one operation. This is because, unlike BondFractions, where if a bond type was missing from the training set you could just ignore it or approximate it, with BoB there are also problems if any structure in the test set has a larger bag than the corresponding bag in the training set. 

Basically what I am saying is that in some circumstances, if you fit on one dataset and featurize a different one, you run into trouble whether you decide to correct it or not. If you don't correct it, you have errors often. If you do correct it, you will either have an incomplete BoB vector or you will have to refit BoB!

Actually never mind. We figured out a way to go Route 1 without modifying graphene code (and Route 2 will change a lot of APIs, which will be very risky).

The basic idea is, you can use a private field in the type (we use `_complexity`), something like:

```python
class MyType(graphene.ObjectType):
    field1 = ...
    field2 = ...
    ...

    _complexity = {
        'field1': 2,
    }
```

Then, in the complexity analysis code, you can get that map back from the schema by something like `schema.get_query_type().fields['fieldName'].graphene_type._complexity`.

In the end I just did more direct filtering in the query resolution method:

```
all_occurrence_assigned_from_to = graphene.List(
    OccurrenceNode, 
    from_date=graphene.String(), 
    to_date=graphene.String(), 
    assigned_to_id=graphene.Int()
)
```

```
def resolve_all_occurrence_assigned_from_to(self, info, **kwargs):
    return Occurrence.objects.filter(
        start_time__gte=dateparse.parse_datetime(kwargs['from_date']),
        end_time__lte=dateparse.parse_datetime(kwargs['to_date']),
        event__assigned_to__id=kwargs['assigned_to_id']
    )
```

These filters are all that's really required, it'd have been nice to have some others that come with django_filters, but I'll leave them out for now.

The cause is here:
https://github.com/django/django/blob/2.1/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L740-L745

When setting up the `DeferredAttribute` descriptors, the case of the base class already having the attribute defined is explicitly unhandled, so deferring fields doesn't work in this case. I guess in this case, we would want to use some custom descriptor, though I'm guessing that would run into whatever problem caused Django to not support deferred fields in the first place. 

I'm not quite sure the right way to fix this, but hopefully that extra bit of information helps out. 🤔

The major move to use yield and generator is to tackle excel files that has huge data. At the moment, pyexcel 0.2.0 gives performance boost only to pyexcel.save_as and pyexcel.save_book_as. For other operations, there is no performance improvement yet. 

Yes, I agree with you that to return a list is the only way to know the length. Hence, pyexcel 0.2.0 [converts generator to an array](https://github.com/pyexcel/pyexcel/blob/master/pyexcel/sheets/matrix.py#L492) when get_sheet and get_book are called. That's why matrix in 0.2.0 won't break.

@jcoatgoogle Just to clarify terminology, we maintain a set of auto-generated gRPC client libraries, GAPIC client libraries. GAX is the runtime dependency of the GAPIC client libraries. GAX itself doesn't contain any special utilities for setting the metadata.

The way you'd do it for [the same method in the GAPIC library](https://github.com/googleapis/api-client-staging/blob/master/generated/python/gapic-google-cloud-datastore-v1/google/cloud/gapic/datastore/v1/datastore_api.py#L141) looks pretty similar to what you're already doing, since GAPIC libraries pass the value of the `metadata` keyword directly through to the gRPC layer.

```
from google.cloud.gapic.datastore.v1 import datastore_api
from google.gax import CallOptions
api = datastore_api.DatastoreApi()
...
api.lookup(project_id, read_options, keys, CallOptions(metadata=[(headerkey, headervalue)]))
```

network = regression(fc3, optimizer='momentum',

```python
transfer_asset_id = tx['id']
transfer_asset = {'id': transfer_asset_id,}
```

That way, those two variables will stay the same, as they should, because the asset id doesn't change.

Note that `include/pybind11/` is different from the location where distutils' `install_headers` directive places headers. It will be the same directory in most cases, but not in other such as with the homebrew python installation. This is the reason why we use the `pip.location` module to get this information.

Also, I would advise against having two copies of the headers in the installation.

The point of the PR pointed to by @dean0x7d was to generate the cmake project files **without** changing the actual installation directory. I did this so that users of the conda package can both use the cmake and python examples consistently.

I don't think that there is a perfect answer to this quite yet in the current state of Python packaging, and that the current approach in pybind makes the best of the state of the tools.

There is a PEP opened to add installation directories: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0491/#install-paths

It's not a problem I have, it's a corner case I discovered because @wjakob asked for my Unicode documentation to mention character literals.

I agree that modern C++11 code is unlikely to accept `char` literals but there's lots of old stuff out there. I found a practical example. I contribute to tesseract-ocr which was written in ancient C++ but is gradually being modernized. Take a quick look at its internal `STRING` class:

https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/blob/d55f462c9c57b9fe5c3c146e126b50ed72090a3d/ccutil/strngs.h

The interface has a several functions like `STRING::split()` and `operator+` that accept `char` literals. Even though this is ancient C++, if I were going to modernize this code, my strategy would be to wrap it with pybind11, write some test cases, modernize the implementation to wrap `std::string` internally, and then modernize the interface to phase it out.

We've established that the pybind11 behavior is incorrect in that it mangles UTF-8, but it is a corner case. I agree it may not even be worth fully fixing, given that `char*` is much more important.

Do you think it would be possible to have a `static_assert` that rejects functions with a `char` or 16-bit wide single character in their signature? 32-bit wide characters work fine, so functions that bind to Python could use that as the literal type and implement a little wrapper to convert in whatever way the caller thinks is appropriate.
I think adding `data()` would also require adding a stride(n) method; without the stride, I don't see any use for direct access to the data() pointer.  But that's easy enough to add.

I don't think this approach is going to be viable for the pybind11 test suite itself. It tests more than just the `pybind11_add_module` function and the targets are tricky (an extra target + test executable is also going to be needed for embedding the interpreter -- upcoming PR).

But perhaps it's better to look outside of testing and distributing pybind11 itself. On the user side, for building and distributing Python extension modules with `pip` and `conda`, different versions must always be built independently, so that's also not the use case.

I believe that there are use cases for fully custom builds (non-Python packaging), but I'm wondering: What does this build look like? That would help inform the design of the feature inside pybind11's build system.

Hi folks :wave:, leaving a comment here for any future wayward souls that discover this thread.

We recently changed the at-registration-time email validation in Boulder, the Let's Encrypt server-side CA, to [treat DNS lookups as best-effort](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/pull/2288) rather than failing on timeout. I suspect this will start alleviating the symptom folks in this thread are experiencing. 

The root cause lies not with Certbot or Boulder and is indicative of (one or more of) your authoritative DNS servers responding slowly for the MX and A/AAAA lookups done to try and validate the email address.

If folks continue to run into this they should open an [issue on the Boulder repo](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/issues/) so we can help troubleshoot there. Please include your domain/email address (we can of-course discuss alternate communication methods if you're unhappy sharing email/domains in Github tickets).

Thanks!

Re: 77faa4b:
It turns out that the problem with calling `pyta_statistics` on a `directory` with multiple student directories inside it was that `importlib` would be trying to look for modules in directories specified in the PYTHONPATH, and `directory` wasn't in the PYTHONPATH. So appending `directory` to `sys.path` was a simple fix (no temporary `__init__.py` files required).
(Actually, the [pylint docs](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide/run.html#invoking-pylint) hint at this, but not very helpfully.)

One thing I'm not sure of: If a user types in module name without `""`, or the user input the wrong type as the module name, for example, `pyta.check(3)`, should we rewrite the error message as well? Or it's their responsibility to know the right parameter type?

Oh, thanks for the stack!

Indeed, the ``factory.Iterator`` / ``factory.iterator`` functions work best with a generator function.
It should work with:

```python
@factory.iterator
def school():
    yield from Session.query(models.School).all()
```

Let's dive under the hood here: a generator function (anything containing ``yield``) returns a special object, which will only execute the function's code (up to the next ``yield`` statement) once ``next(x)`` is called on it.

Django's querysets are built upon generators, and work natively; however, it seems that SQLAlchemy's querysets will evaluate directly; using a ``yield from x`` is equivalent to ``for i in x: yield i``, and "wraps" the call in a generator.

This could be fixed either through better docs, or in the code; I'll have to see which way feels most natural.


#### Regarding the lack of ``self``:

There is some "dark magic" occurring in factory_boy: basically, the engine will collect all attributes from your class definition, and store them in some internal structures for later use.

I use the ``self`` parameter to calls because it feels more natural, but that's actually a trick :wink:
When creating an object, a *method* of those definitions is called with a stub for the attributes of the object to be built.
Once that stub is filled, its attributes are used as kwargs for the actual model.

Since an iterator cannot be tuned for the object being built (whereas a ``lazy_attribute`` is designed for that!), there is no ``self`` parameter.

One possibility is the following:

- We notify a local scheduler that it is supposed to create an actor with a redis command like `PUBLISH actor_notifications <actor_id><local_scheduler_id>`.
- The local scheduler subscribes to this channel and passes in a callback that creates the actor in response to a message arriving on the channel.
- However, if the publish happens before the subscription is in place, then the actor will never be created and an invocation of one of the actor's methods will never execute.

If this is the problem, then it's part of a recurring problem in which we're using Redis's pubsub functionality as a message bus, but when a client subscribes to a channel, it only receives messages that are sent after the subscription. If it wants to receive previous messages, another mechanism or a layer is needed.

We could enumerate the various function types we want to support (maybe a catch-all ```is_ray_func``` and ```is_ray_method```), but @robertnishihara's workaround in #593 is similar to what we have to do for Cython anyway (though, admittedly, their use cases are a different with numba much more likely used in driver code).

If folks are into it, I could do this pretty quickly, but I'm not sure if 1) the workaround is painful enough and 2) enumerating function types (absent some better mechanism) is desirable.

Hi,

when you specify the condition as you do, you are getting the articles that mention in the dateline (the beginning of the article) some city in Italy. If you simply want to find articles that mention some specific location then you should use conceptUri or ignoreConceptUri if you want to exclude articles that mention that location. If you use the exclusion parameters then you also need to have some positive conditions, otherwise the query won't be valid. 

Do you have some valid use case where specifying article location would make sense as an exclusion parameter? If it's a generally useful use case we can add that functionality.

Best,
Gregor

Hi,

there are over 5000 categories so we don't have an online list of all of them. You can however download the URIs for all the categories from here:

https://www.dropbox.com/s/dysl0xa3noksk2s/dmoz-categories.txt?dl=0

You should know that the categories are hierarchical - if you, for example, ignore category dmoz/Arts this will also ignore all it's subcategories (i.e. dmoz/Arts/Animation).

Regarding the concepts, we don't have a list of concept URIs. Concepts URIs are URLs of the English Wikipedia. So for example, for Barack Obama, the corresponding URI would be "http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barack_Obama" (notice that we use http://.. and not https://...). 
To get the URIs for the concept given a name, you can also use the autosuggestion methods -
 see here: http://eventregistry.org/documentation?tab=suggConcepts

With the latest commit the following works:

```python
from tensorpack import *

class SomeOldDf(DataFlow):
    """docstring for SomeOldDf"""

    def __init__(self):
        super(SomeOldDf, self).__init__()

    def size(self):
        return 2

    def get_data(self):
        for dp in [1, 2]:
            yield [dp]


class SomeNewDf(DataFlow):
    """docstring for SomeNewDf"""

    def __init__(self):
        super(SomeNewDf, self).__init__()

    def __len__(self):
        return 2

    def __iter__(self):
        for dp in [1, 2]:
            yield [dp]


ds = SomeOldDf()
ds = BatchData(ds, 2)
print(next(ds.__iter__()))
print(next(ds.__iter__()))
print(ds.size())
print(len(ds))

ds = SomeOldDf()
ds = BatchData(ds, 2)
print(next(ds.get_data()))
print(next(ds.get_data()))
print(ds.size())
print(len(ds))

ds = SomeNewDf()
ds = BatchData(ds, 2)
print(next(ds.__iter__()))
print(next(ds.__iter__()))
print(ds.size())
print(len(ds))

ds = SomeNewDf()
ds = BatchData(ds, 2)
print(next(ds.get_data()))
print(next(ds.get_data()))
print(ds.size())
print(len(ds))
```

I do understand that this verification may be a little bit outside the original scope of rdopkg and that in this case we may want to enable it with an optional parameter instead of having to introspect the stdout/stderr output in order to make a decision if it was a genuine failure or not. The findCaller() API comes from the CPython standard library logging
module.  In 2.7 it returns a three-tuple.  In 3.x is returns a four-tuple.
  https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L1240
vs https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L1375

The stdlib logging.Logger base class itself expects those semantics when it
calls findCaller, other code may as well.

@tarak77 I might also note that since you are providing a contact matrix, you should flag to PHATE that the distance matrix is precomputed.

In Python:
```
>>> import phate
>>> contact_map = ...
>>> contact_map.shape
(n, n)
>>> phate_operator = phate.PHATE(knn_dist='precomputed')
>>> contact_phate = phate_operator.fit_transform(contact_map)
```

In R:
```
> library(phateR)
> contact_map <- ...
> dim(contact_map)
[1] n n
> contact_phate <- phate(contact_map, knn.dist='precomputed')
```

Ok, so where we're at:

1. This pull request supports local maven support.  Presumably this is useful to many people/projects?
2. I am not against this pull request since it doesn't mess with setup.py's ease of use for building.
3. We can't seem to get on central maven without owning the domain name, which is not available.  https://issues.sonatype.org/browse/OSSRH-34206  I suggest we defer for now on any attempts to get there.  ([ninia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ninia) is a coffeesnake since it's not obvious).
4. I'm somewhat indifferent on the IDE files, it's not clear to me how useful they are to developers using those IDES.  There's no way @bsteffensmeier and I can maintain them so they may become obsolete.
5. I can work on a fork or branch to try and get the source and test folders structured like maven expects, while keeping setup.py building and testing.  I'm not sure when I can get to that.  At that time we'd need help to update the pom.xml but it should be simpler then?
6. I still like the idea of the jar just being named jep-3.7.0.jar.  If someone doesn't build the native library, they just get UnsatisfiedLinkError.  We have no Java dependencies (and intend to keep it that way), it's just a jar and a native library.  I specifically made setup.py build a jar following open source naming conventions.

Presuming a decent number of people will find this local maven support useful, I support it and am thankful @ananthc is taking the time to get it to work.

A check that gets even more (than just the SX):

```python
x = ca.SX.sym('x')
y = ca.SX.sym('y')
z = ca.SX.sym('z')

f = x * (y - 2 * z)
g = x * (y - 2 * z)

# Not good enough yet
f - g  # SX(@1=2, ((x*(y-(@1*z)))-(x*(y-(@1*z)))))
(f - g).is_zero()  # False
# Expanding also does not work
f_exp = ca.Function('tmp', [x, y, z], [f - g]).expand()
f_exp(x, y, z)  # SX(@1=2, ((x*(y-(@1*z)))-(x*(y-(@1*z)))))

# What does work is using the pickling/serializing on SXFunctions
f_f = ca.Function('tmp', [x, y, z], [f])
f_g = ca.Function('tmp', [x, y, z], [g])

f_f.serialize() == f_g.serialize()  # True
```

Imagine you built the parser without an error, and then wrote `parse('a')`. What parse-tree would you expect as the output? More specifically, which rule do you expect the parser to match?

Well, turns out, both `one` and `many` would be "correct" derivations. But, LALR is a strictly deterministic algorithm, and it refuses to make a guess when faced with ambiguity. Instead, it throws an error.

So you have two options:

1) Change the grammar, so that there's only one correct path to take for each input.

2) Use Earley, which will happily accept ambiguous grammars.

It's a good tip, but I want to see the browser's window when a problem happens, for example: I have chromium instead of Chrome and it is not fully compatible with chromedirver; because of it, Splinter can't click in buttons on alerts(). If I can't know what's happening, my tests will just freeze and if I can't see the problem.

So, I prefer to use __Xephyr__ instead of `Xvfb` -- it looks like Xvfb but the entire X server is openned in an windows -- so you can see what's happening and interact.

I've created a script to run tests using Xephyr that is safe when killing the Xephyr process (it does not use killall since it can kill other instances of Xephyr that are not related to Splinter tests). It also outputs Xephyr's stderror to /dev/null. The script is in my fork: [run_tests_within_Xephyr.sh](https://github.com/turicas/splinter/blob/master/run_tests_within_Xephyr.sh)

I think with this script we finish this issue -- it'll be good if someday Selenium changes its behaviour in GNU/Linux, but for now the problem is completly solved with Xephyr.
